segments.s667-g433: خروشان is an adjectival/participle used as a substantive meaning "the roaring/tumultuous ones." ازان is an older fused form of از آن meaning "from that" and, with جایگه "place," forms the prepositional phrase "from that place." برنشست is the past tense (3rd person) of برنشستن (بر + نشست); in classical Persian a singular past often stands for a group action, so here it means "rose up/stood up/left their place."
segments.s667-g434: یکی here means "one" or "a certain one" (one of them) and functions as the subject of the second clause; the verb is omitted (elliptical) so the noun phrase describes that person. گرزه is the head noun "mace/cudgel," modified by the following compound گاو پیکر (گاو + پیکر = "cow-bodied/with the body of a cow"), where the modifier follows the noun in classical style and the ezāfe is omitted in script. به دست is a prepositional phrase meaning "in (his) hand/by hand," indicating possession of the mace.
segments.s667-g113: به is a preposition here marking the means or manner ('by/with'); گفتار is the noun 'speech' and is governed by به. بی + سود is an adjectival phrase 'without benefit / profitless' modifying گفتار, and و links the coordinated complement دیوانگی ('folly, madness') — together: 'by profitless speech and folly.'
segments.s667-g114: نجوید is a 3rd-person present (negative) verb meaning 'does not seek' or 'should not seek' and here precedes its nominal subject (جهانجوی, a compound 'world-seeker' = ambitious person) in typical poetic V‑initial order; مردانگی is the object 'valor/bravery.'
segments.s667-g115: فرستاده is a noun 'the sent one / messenger' followed by مرد ('man') as an appositive/identifier; گردنفراز is an attributive epithet 'neck‑raising,' i.e. 'proud, haughty,' placed after the head noun in the usual Persian adjective position.
segments.s667-g116: بیامد is the past tense 'came.' به نزدیک + بهرام is a prepositional phrase 'to/near Bahram' (به marks direction/goal), and باز is an adverb 'again' placed after the phrase — literal order: 'came to near Bahram again.'
segments.s667-g117: بگفت is a past verb 'he said' (reported speech). آن گزاینده is a nominal phrase 'that chooser' (an epithet); پیغام اوی is a possessed noun 'his message' (پیغام = message, اوی = his/of him) with the possessive following the noun as usual.
segments.s667-g118: همانا is an emphatic 'indeed/verily.' که introduces the content clause. بد is a predicative adjective 'bad/ill'; زان is an ablative/prepositional 'from that' modifying سخن 'speech'; کام اوی is 'his desire/satisfaction' (کام = desire, اوی = his). The clause says roughly 'indeed, his desire is ill / will be ill from that speech.'
segments.s667-g119: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when'); با here marks accompaniment/participant 'with/by the man' and مرد گوینده identifies 'the man who was speaking' or 'the speaking man'; گفت is the main verb 'said.' The clause as a whole: 'When the man heard the speaker, he said...' (poetic word order places the temporal clause and participant before the main verb).
segments.s667-g120: که introduces the reported content ('that'). پاسخ is 'answer/reply.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and مهتر is 'the chief/leader'; نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not / should not,' and نهفت is the passive/participial verb 'be hidden / be kept back.' Together: 'that an answer must not be hidden from the chief.'
segments.s667-g33: The sentence begins with the topical subject pronoun من (I). ایدون is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly ‘now / here / at this time’ and frames the action; آمدم is a past‑tense first‑person verb ‘I came’ (آمدم = I came). The prepositional phrase به رزم (‘to/for battle’) follows the verb, and با سپاه (‘with an army’) is an instrumental accompaniment placed after the verb.
segments.s667-g34: ز بغداد رفتم is a past‑tense clause meaning ‘I left/ I went from Baghdad’: ز indicates origin (‘from’) and رفتم is the 1st‑person past form ‘I went/left’ (the subject ‘I’ is pronominally encoded in the verb). به فرمان شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘by/at the king’s command’ (به introduces the agency/authority). Word order is typical: origin + verb, then cause/authority.
segments.s667-g35: چو is a subordinating particle here meaning ‘when’. از introduces the source ‘from (the army of…)’ and لشکر ساوه‑شاه is the noun phrase ‘the army/host of the king of Sāveh’. آگهی is the noun ‘news / information’ and is the head of the phrase; overall this is a temporal clause ‘when news from the army of the Sāveh‑king…’.
segments.s667-g36: بیامد is a past tense verb ‘came’. بدان is the fused prepositional phrase (ب + آن = ‘to that’) meaning ‘to/into that’, used here before the noun. بارگاه مهی is the noun + adjective ‘that resplendent/majestic court’ (مهی is a poetic adjective). The whole segment means ‘came to that resplendent court.’
segments.s667-g37: مرا گفت literally ‘(he) said to me’: مرا is the object ‘me’ + object marker (here split in the tokens), and گفت is the past‑tense verb ‘said / told’. The following clause رو راه ایشان بگیر is an imperative addressed to someone: رو (= toward) + راه (‘road/way’) + ایشان (‘them’) and بگیر is the 2nd‑person imperative ‘take’ (i.e. ‘take the road toward them’). Word order places the reported‑speech verb before the quoted imperative.
segments.s667-g38: This is a coordinate list of weapons introduced by به in an instrumental sense (‘with/by’): به گرز و سنان و به شمشیر و تیر = ‘with mace and spear, and with sword and arrow’. Repetition of به is a poetic parallelism; و simply links items.
segments.s667-g39: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. بشنید is the past‑tense verb ‘he heard’. فغفور is the subject ‘the emperor’. برگشت is the past tense ‘returned’ and زود is an adverb ‘quickly/soon’. The clause reads ‘When he heard, the emperor returned quickly.’ Note the simple past narrative sequence.
segments.s667-g40: به پیش پدر شد is an idiomatic expression ‘he went before/into the presence of his father’ (به پیش = to the presence of). شد is a light verb here marking the event (‘he came/went’). بگفت is a past‑tense reporting verb ‘he said / told’, and آنچه بود is a relative phrase ‘what had happened/that which was (the case)’ with بود as past. The full meaning: ‘he went to his father and told him what had happened.’
segments.s667-g1: ز گفتار او شاد شد ساوه شاه is a compact narrative clause: ز (a poetical preposition) introduces cause/source (“from / because of his speech”), گفتار او is the genitival phrase “his speech,” شاد شد is the past (perfective) verb phrase “became glad,” and ساوه شاه (Saveh’s king) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order — typical of classical Persian where subject can follow the predicate.
segments.s667-g2: بدو گفت مانا که اینست راه: بدو is the dative pronoun “to him,” گفت here functions as the reporting verb “said” (the lemma senses provided lack a matching verbal sense, see senses), مانا is used vocatively/parenthetically (here treated as a name or vocative), که introduces the quoted content or a that‑clause, and اینست راه is the demonstrative copular phrase “this is the way.” The sequence is a direct speech/report structure where the dative precedes the verb (typical older Persian ordering).
segments.s667-g3: چو indicates a temporal subordinate clause “when.” خراد here functions as an epithet/adjective “wise” modifying the proper name برزین (Barzin); سوی خانه رفت is the verb phrase “went toward the house” with سوی (toward) + noun + simple past رفت. The clause order (temporal marker + epithet + name + direction + verb) is normal in narrative poetry.
segments.s667-g4: برآمد شب تیره از کوه تفت: برآمد is the intransitive past “arose/issued forth,” شب تیره is the noun + adjective “dark night” (adjective after noun), and از introduces origin “from” followed by کوه تفت ‘Mount Taft.’ The whole clause is a simple nominative description of something emerging (a night) from a place.
segments.s667-g5: بسیجید و برساخت راه گریز contains two coordinated verbs joined by و: بسیجید (they mustered/assembled) and برساخت (they built/constructed). راه گریز is the object phrase “a route of escape” (راه = path, گریز = escape) placed after the verbs. The verbs are narrative perfect/past forms used to relate events in sequence.
segments.s667-g6: بدان تا نیاید بدو رستخیز explains purpose/result: دان here combines with the preceding prepositional element (بدان = ‘to/for that’ or ‘for that purpose’), تا introduces the final clause “so that/that,” نیاید is a negative-present/subjunctive sense “should not come” (expressing prevention), بدو is the dative “to him,” and رستخیز is the noun “uprising” (here: hostile rising). Overall the phrase marks the intent of their action — they prepared the escape route in order to prevent an uprising from reaching him.
segments.s667-g7: بدانگه که شب تیره‌تر گشت شاه: بدانگه is a temporal/conjunctive adverb “thereupon/at that moment,” که introduces the subordinate clause “when,” شب تیره‌تر گشت is a predicate with comparative تیره‌تر and past گشت ‘became/darkened,’ and شاه (the king) appears as the subject. Word order (adverbial phrase + que-clause + predicate + subject) reflects poetic flexibility.
segments.s667-g8: به فغفور فرمود تا بی‌سپاه: به + recipient (فغفور, the Byzantine emperor) marks the indirect object/dative “to the emperor,” فرمود is the reporting/command verb (here used as an order), تا introduces the content/purpose clause “that/so that” (again used to introduce the commanded action), and بی‌سپاه is the predicate complement “without an army” — the verb of arrival/coming is implied and omitted, a common concise/polished tactic in epic narrative.
segments.s667-g161: همی‌بود is a poetic imperfect/progressive (همی + بود) meaning “it was continuing” or “it kept on being.” تا introduces a temporal clause here = “until.” آن سپه شارستان is a nominal phrase “that army (آن سپه) [took] the town (شارستان)” placed before the verb (common in poetry) — شارستان is a poetic word for “town/city.”
segments.s667-g162: گرفتند is past, 3rd person plural “they seized/took.” و links the coordinated clause. شد is the past of شدن “became.” جای من خارستان is a verbless predicate cluster inverted for poetic effect: literally “place (جای) of me (من) [a] thorny-thicket (خارستان),” i.e. “my place became a thorny thicket.” The usual prose order would be جای من خارستان شد.
segments.s667-g163: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + آن' meaning “to/at that” (poetic contraction). جای تنگی = “that narrow/cramped place” (تنگی = narrowness/constriction). صفی برکشید = “they drew up a line/formed a rank” where برکشید is past (3rd sing./pl.) “raised/drew up” and صفی (here from صف) is the object meaning “a rank/line.” Word order places location first, then the action of forming ranks.
segments.s667-g164: هوا نیلگون شد is a normal predicative clause: noun (هوا) + adjective (نیلگون) + copula (شد) = “the sky/air became blue.” زمین ناپدید is elliptical: the verb is omitted for brevity (common in poetry), so “the earth vanished” (ناپدید used predicatively); understood verb is شد or similar.
segments.s667-g165: سپه بود is past copula “there was an army.” بر میمنه is a locative phrase “on the right (side)” (میمنه = right side). چل هزار is an archaic numeral phrase “forty thousand” (چل = چهل, used poetically before هزار). The number follows the locative here for poetic word order.
segments.s667-g166: که introduces a clause explaining/resulting from the previous statement (“so that/that”). تنگ آمدش is an idiomatic impersonal construction: تنگ آمد + indirect object suffix ش (to him/it) = “it became tight for him” / “he found it cramped.” جای خنجرگزار = “the place for (the) dagger-bearer” (خنجرگزار either literal ‘dagger-bearer’ or a pejorative epithet). The whole clause: “so that the place became cramped for the dagger-bearer.”
segments.s667-g167: همان is emphatic/demonstrative “that very / those same.” چل هزار از دلیران مرد is a partitive expression “forty thousand men from the brave (ones)” — از + group indicates 'of/from' that class. مرد here is a noun “men/warriors” following the partitive.
segments.s667-g168: پس is adverbial “then/afterwards.” پشت لشکرش = “the rear/behind of his army” (پشت = rear, لشکرش = his army). بر پای کرد is a compound verbal phrase where بر پای (on foot/base) + کرد “made/caused” = “set up/placed/caused to stand” — here meaning they took up position as the rear-guard. کرد is past 3rd singular.
segments.s667-g57: تو را گفت is a terse, poetic order meaning “(he) said to you”: تو is the (emphatic) second‑person pronoun and را is the object marker attached to it. What follows (رو راه بر من بگیر) is an imperative quotation: رو here marks direction/toward (literally “face/turn toward”), راه is “road/way,” بر من = “toward me,” and بگیر is the 2nd‑person imperative “take” (i.e. ‘take the road toward me’). Word order is somewhat compressed and archaic/poetic.
segments.s667-g58: شنیدی is the past tense verb (“you heard”); the subject تو follows the verb for emphasis (verb‑subject order is common in older/poetic Persian). گفتار نادلپذیر is a noun phrase, with گفتار “speech/words” qualified by the adjective نادلپذیر “unpleasant/unacceptable.” The whole segment states that you heard that disagreeable utterance.
segments.s667-g59: اگر introduces a conditional clause (“if”). کوه نزد من آید به راه literally “if a mountain comes near me on the road”: نزد = “near/to (the presence of),” آید is the 3rd‑person present (classical/predictive use ‘comes’), and به راه indicates the route/way. This is a protasis stating a hypothetical arrival.
segments.s667-g60: به پای اندر آرم is the apodosis: a literal “I put (it) into/at the foot” — آرم is 1st‑person singular (classical form) meaning “I will place / I set” (a present/optative sense used for future result). به before پای marks placement/direction (“to/at the foot”), اندر = “within/at,” and به پیل و سپاه lists the instruments/forces (“with elephants and an army”) — Persian here uses به + nouns to indicate accompaniment or means in a poetic register.
segments.s667-g61: چو بشنید introduces a temporal clause “when (he) heard.” بشنید is past tense “he heard” and the subject (بهرام) follows it in this poetic order; گفتار اوی is the object phrase “his speech/words” (اوی = his).
segments.s667-g62: بخندید is past tense “(he) laughed.” زان is a demonstrative/relational particle “thereupon/at that,” linking this reaction to the preceding words. تیز modifies بازار (“sharp/keen market/utterance”); here تیز بازار اوی idiomatically means the keenness or sharpness of his speech/repartee. اوی again is the possessive “his.”
segments.s667-g63: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed, slightly archaic phrase meaning “he gave such an answer” or “thus answered.” چنین (“thus/so”) modifies داد پاسخ (“gave an answer”); که then introduces the content of that answer (subordinate or reported speech). شاه جهان is a title‑like genitive phrase “king of the world.”
segments.s667-g64: اگر ... جوید اندر نهان is a conditional clause: اگر = “if,” جوید is present/subjunctive “(should) seek” or “seeks” in a hypothetical sense, and اندر نهان = “in secret/hiddenly.” The order is protasis (if‑clause) giving the condition under which the rest of the reply follows.
segments.s667-g329: همی here is a classical/perfective particle giving an ongoing or emphatic past sense before the verb; رای زد is a compound where رای (counsel/plan) is the object and زد is the past tense verb 'struck/made' used idiomatically to mean 'decided/held council'. جنگ را is marked by the object marker را and names what was decided; با سپاه indicates the means or accompaniment ('with an army').
segments.s667-g330: برینگونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this way/so'; تا introduces the result or extent ('so that/until'); گشت is the 3rd‑person past of گشتن used here as 'became/turned'; گیتی سیاه is the predicative phrase 'the world (giti) black', with سیاه describing گیتی. The clause therefore expresses result: 'in this way so that the world became black.'
segments.s667-g331: بخفتند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense meaning literally 'they slept' but in epic usage often implies 'they fell (were laid low/put to rest)'—context shows it as a past event affecting the groups named. ترکان is the plural subject 'the Turks'; و links to the following noun; پر مایگان is a compound/juxtaposition meaning 'winged (پر) creatures/multitudes (مایگان)', i.e. 'the feathered or winged hosts.'
segments.s667-g332: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد as perfective 'became'). جهانجویی is a verbal noun meaning 'world‑seeking' or the act/person who seeks worldly gain; را marks that phrase as the (logical) object/topic of the following adjective رایگان 'free'. The clause means that the pursuit of worldly gain became free/open (i.e. easily had).
segments.s667-g333: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the proper name. جنگی is an adjectival epithet 'warlike/warrior' attached to the name. به خیمه is a locative phrase 'in/into the tent' and بخفت is the 3rd‑person past 'he slept'—together a temporal subordinate clause 'when Bahram, the warrior, slept in the tent.'
segments.s667-g334: همه شب is an adverbial phrase 'all night'. دلش is دل 'heart' plus the enclitic ش 'his'. بود is the past copula 'was'. با جنگ جفت is a predicative phrase where با جنگ 'with battle' and جفت 'paired' (metaphorically 'matched/attuned'); together 'his heart was paired with battle' (i.e. he was all night full of warlike feeling).
segments.s667-g335: A poetic, slightly inverted word order: چنان modifies the seeing (دید) and introduces a result clause with که. در خواب is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in (his) sleep/dream', and بهرام is the subject while شیر is the direct object. In smoother prose this would be بَهرام در خواب چنین شِیر دید — here the verb (دید) comes early for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.s667-g336: که introduces the result clause 'that ...'. ترکان is the plural subject 'the Turks'. شدندی is an archaic/poetic past-plural form meaning 'they became' (modern شدند). به + noun here marks the domain ('in/for the battle of ...'), and جنگ‌ش is 'his battle/war' with the enclitic -ش referring back to Bahram. دلیر is the predicate adjective 'brave' following the verb, so the clause says the Turks 'became brave in his battle' (i.e. brave to fight him).
segments.s667-g49: 'وزان پس' is an archaic/poetic temporal frame: 'وزان' = 'from that (time)' and 'پس' = 'then/after that'. 'فرستاد' is the past-tense verb 'he sent' and governs the following object phrase 'مرد کهن' ('an old/aged man'). Word order is Subject-Verb-Object here with the temporal adverbial up front; 'مرد کهن' is a noun followed by its adjective.
segments.s667-g50: This prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک بهرام' means 'to (the) near/nearby [place] of Bahram' i.e. 'to Bahram's presence.' 'چیره سخن' is an adjective-noun phrase with the adjective after the noun in elevated/poetic word order: 'چیره' ('skilled, masterful') modifies 'سخن' ('speech'), so it describes the man as 'masterful in speech.'
segments.s667-g51: 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative), followed by 'گفت' (said) — the phrase functions as 'he said to him'. 'رو پارسی را بگوی' is an imperative clause: 'رو پارسی' = 'the Persian tongue/manner' (object), 'را' marks it as definite/object, and 'بگوی' is the imperative 'speak/say.' Word order is verb-final for the imperative phrase, with the object preceding 'را' then the verb.
segments.s667-g52: The particle 'که' introduces the following command/explanation ('that/so that'). 'ایدر' = 'here.' 'به' is a preposition ('to/onto'), and 'خیره' describes the person as 'staring/astonished.' 'مریز' is the negative imperative 'do not spill/pour,' governing its object 'آب' ('water'), with final 'روی' = 'on the face.' The clause literally orders: 'do not pour water on the staring face here.'
segments.s667-g53: 'همانا' is an emphatic particle meaning 'verily/truly.' The following 'که' introduces the content clause. 'این مایه' = 'this cause/resource' (مایه used here = 'cause'), and 'دانی' is the verb from دانستن addressed to the addressee ('know' / 'you should know'); 'درست' qualifies the clause as 'correct/true.' Overall: 'Truly know this cause to be correct.'
segments.s667-g54: This is the explanatory clause introduced by کزین (= 'from this'). 'پادشاه تو' = 'this king of yours' (genitive), and 'مرگ تو جست' is a past action: 'your death leaped/sprang (forth).' The verb 'جست' is perfective/past 'sprang,' so the clause says that from this king your death has sprung.
segments.s667-g55: 'به جنگت' = 'to your war/battle' (prepositional phrase with possessive -ت). 'فرستاد' is the past-tense main verb 'he sent.' 'نزد کسی' ('to/unto someone') uses 'نزد' in the sense 'to the presence of/unto,' so together the phrase means 'he sent (someone) to your battle.'
segments.s667-g56: The relative clause 'که همتا ندارد به گیتی بسی' modifies that someone: 'که' = 'who/that.' 'همتا ندارد' = 'has no peer/equal.' 'به گیتی' = 'in the world' (prepositional phrase), and 'بسی' here is an intensifying/old adverb (literally 'much/enough'), giving force to 'has no equal.' Word order is typical classical Persian for a relative clause after the head noun.
segments.s667-g193: سلیحست is an older/poetic combination of the noun سلیح (weapons/armour) plus the copula ـست (est), functioning existentially or predicatively (‘there is / is armed’). The following و links items in a list: خرگاه (stable/pen for beasts of burden) and پرده سرای (a curtained chamber or pavilion) — note پرده modifies سرای (a place/room), so the two words form a compound noun phrase.
segments.s667-g194: فزون here means ‘more / besides’; زانک is a subordinating conjunction ‘because / for’; اندیشه is the subject ‘thought / the mind’; آرد is 3rd-person singular present of آوردن ‘brings’ (classical present used to state a general fact). به جای is an adverbial/locative phrase ‘into place / in place’, so the clause says that the mind brings (them) into being or arranges them in place.
segments.s667-g195: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from / of’ introducing the things to be reckoned. The sequence اسبان و مردان بیابان و کوه lists two kinds of beings (horses and men) qualified by two locations (the waste/desert and the mountain); modifiers follow the nouns in this classical word order so ‘horses and men of the wilderness and the mountain’.
segments.s667-g196: اگر introduces a hypothetical (‘if’). بشمرد is the 3rd-person singular subjunctive/jussive of شمردن (here ‘were to count / should count’), نیـز is the adverb ‘also / even’, and گردد (from گشتن) is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive meaning ‘become’. ستوه is a predicative adjective ‘weary / sick of’. Together they form the conditional result: ‘if he counts …, he too will become weary.’
segments.s667-g197: همه ‘all’ quantifies شهریاران (plural ‘kings / rulers’). The sequence م + را in the manuscript correspond to the dative/object pronoun مرا (‘to me’ / ‘for me’); here it signals comparison/target (‘to me’). کهترند is 3rd-person plural ‘are lesser / inferior’. The literal word order is ‘All kings to me are lesser.’ (Note: the tokenization shown splits مرا into م and را; grammatically they form the pronoun plus object marker.)
segments.s667-g198: اگر introduces a conditional (‘if’). کهتری را ‘the lesser / inferiority’ is the object (را marks it as definite/ topical). خود ‘themselves’ emphasizes the subject performing the action. اندر is an old preposition ‘in / among / within’. خورند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of خوردن; here it is idiomatic for ‘accept / submit to / be subject to’ (literally ‘eat’ but used metaphorically). So: ‘if they themselves accept/consume inferiority.’
segments.s667-g199: اگر marks a counterfactual/hypothetical. گرددی is 2nd-person singular past/subjunctive form meaning ‘if you were to become / if you turned into’. آب دریا is the compound ‘water of the sea’, and روان is an adjective ‘flowing / running’; together they mean ‘if you became the sea’s flowing water’. The clause uses the hypothetical subjunctive to imagine an impossible/metaphorical condition.
segments.s667-g200: وگر ‘but if / and if’ introduces a second impossible condition. کوه را topicalizes ‘the mountain’ (را marking it). پای ‘foot / base’ is the noun whose existence is being supposed. باشد is a classical 3rd-person subjunctive/optative of بودن (‘may be / were there’), here ‘if the mountain had a foot’, and دوان (3rd-person form of دواندن/دواندن) means ‘it would run / it would rush’. The pair expresses an absurdity: even if the mountain had feet, it would run.
segments.s667-g25: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; word order is 'چو + subject (فغفور چینی) + verb phrase'. 'بدیدش' is past tense 'he saw him' with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it'). 'بتاخت' is an archaic/preclassical past verb formed with the prefix بَ- plus تافت/تاخت (to gallop/charge) meaning 'he galloped/charged'.
segments.s667-g26: This clause names the horse and describes what was done to it. segments.s667-g27: 'بپرسید' is a past/factual report 'he asked' (third person past). The connective 'و' links clauses. segments.s667-g28: 'کنون' = 'now' sets the temporal frame. 'ایستاده' is a participle/adjective 'standing' used predicatively. segments.s667-g29: شنیدم is the first-person past tense 'I heard'. که introduces a reported subordinate clause ('that'). از پارس is a prepositional phrase 'from Pars/Persia' placed before the clause-final verb, and بگریختی is the second-person past 'you fled/ran away' (the prefix ب- is a classical/poetic perfective marker). The overall order follows Persian clause structure: subordinate subject/object before the verb, with the verb final.
segments.s667-g30: که again links another subordinate clause ('that'). آزرده is an adjective 'distressed/upset'. گشتی here is a past form meaning 'you became/turned' (classical use of گشتن/شدن to mark change of state) rather than the listed senses; because the provided sense list doesn't include this, it is marked as unknown. وخون ریختی is a coordinated clause with و 'and' + خون 'blood' as object and ریختی the second-person past 'you poured/spilled', i.e., 'you shed blood.' segments.s667-g31: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the speech, and گفت is the verb 'said' (the provided senses list gave 'conversation' rather than 'said', so the exact sense index is not in the list). بهرام is the speaker's name. کین خود مباد is an optative/negative wish: مباد = 'may it not be'; کین خود 'my/one's own hatred' (خود as emphatic/possessive 'own'). Together it is an expression meaning 'may hatred not be mine.'
segments.s667-g32: که introduces the content/clarification of the wish ('that ...'). با شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'with the King of Iran' (با + شاه + ایران). کنم is the first-person present/subjunctive of کردن used here in the optative sense 'that I should do/hold'; کینه یاد is a nominal phrase 'grudge/hatred (کینه) [to] remember/recall/bring to mind (یاد)', i.e. 'to bear/hold a grudge.' Word order places the verb کنَم before its nominal complement in this poetic classical style.
segments.s667-g185: 'چو' introduces a comparative/temporal clause ('when' / 'as'); 'خورشید بر آسمان' = 'the sun on the sky'; 'روشنند' is a present plural predicate 'are bright'. Note the verb is plural here (روشنند) even though خورشید is singular — a common poetic agreement to refer back to an implied plural (the two people previously mentioned) or to give a grand, distributive sense.
segments.s667-g186: 'ز' = 'from/through/because of'; 'مردی' here means 'manliness' or 'the quality of being a man' rather than an individual; 'همه‌ساله' literally 'yearly' (used here as 'ever/continually' in poetic usage); 'در جوشنند' is an archaic/poetic stative construction: جوشن (armor) + copular ending -ند, so the phrase means 'they are in/are clad in armor'.
segments.s667-g187: 'یکی من' = 'one (is) me' (one of the pair is I); 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; 'شاهم' is the copula+noun 'I am king' (شاه + -م); 'جهان را' is marked by the object marker را (here the world is the object of the following notion); 'به داد' literally 'with justice' or 'to justice' — the clause means 'who, as king, gives/rules the world with justice.'
segments.s667-g188: 'دگر' = 'the other/another'; 'نیز' = 'also'; 'فرزند' = 'child/son' (here 'a son/descendant'); 'فرخ نژاد' is a compound epithet 'fortunate-born' or 'of fortunate lineage' (فرخ modifies نژاد), so the whole phrase means 'the other is likewise a scion/son of the fortunate race/lineage.'
segments.s667-g189: 'سپاهم' = 'my army/host' (سپاه + possessive -م); 'فزونتر' is the comparative 'more numerous/more abundant'; 'ز' is the poetic comparative 'than' (literally 'from'); 'برگ درخت' = 'the leaves of the tree' (classical word order without an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s667-g190: 'اگر' introduces a conditional 'if'; 'بشمرد' is the third-person subjunctive/present of شمردن 'to count' (here 'were to count' or 'count'); 'مردم' = 'people' and 'نیکبخت' follows as a postpositive adjective 'fortunate' — together 'اگر مردم نیکبخت بشمرد' = 'if the fortunate people count (them).' Word order places the modifier after the noun in classical style.
segments.s667-g191: 'گراز، پیل و لشکر' are three items listed in sequence ('boar, elephant, and army/host'); 'بگیرم' is first-person singular present/subjunctive of گرفتن 'I take' used here with 'شمار' to mean 'I take the count / I count'; 'شمار' is the noun 'count/number' — together 'گراز پیل و لشکر بگیرم شمار' = 'if I count/take the number of boars, elephants, and the host.'
segments.s667-g192: 'بخندی' (2nd person singular subjunctive/optative) here means 'you would laugh' or 'you will laugh' as the result clause; 'ز' = 'because of / at'; 'باران ابر بهار' = 'the rain of spring clouds' (classical juxtaposition for genitive), a simile/metaphor comparing the multiplicity to a spring shower. Thus, 'you would laugh at them like the rain of spring clouds.'
segments.s667-g313: This is a short reported-speech clause. بدو is the prepositional pronoun meaning "to him/unto him" and precedes the verb (typical SOV flexibility in classical Persian). گفت is the past tense of گفتن "said" and introduces the speech; فغفور (the speaker) follows, so the literal order is 'to him said the Caesar.' کین لابه چیست is the content-question: کین 'vengeance/hatred' modifies لابه 'lament/plea' and چی ست (here written چی ست as two words) is the copular question "what is?" — چی (what) + ست (is).
segments.s667-g314: This is a purposive/result clause that follows the quoted question. بران (بر + ان) = 'on that account / for that' (acting as a connective adverbial). مایه here functions as 'cause / reason' governing the following noun. لشکر is the definite object 'the army' (implicit object of گریست); بباید is the present-tense impersonal/modal form meaning 'one must / it is necessary,' and گریست is an infinitival/past-stem verb meaning 'to mourn/weep' (used here in the sense 'must be mourned' or 'one must lament'). The phrase orders the necessity: 'for that cause the army must be lamented.'
segments.s667-g315: بیامد is the simple past 'he came.' به is the preposition 'to/into' marking direction, here taking دهلیز 'corridor/vestibule' as its object. پرده سرای is a compound describing the room (parde-sarā, 'curtain-chamber' or 'curtained hall/anteroom'); پرده 'curtain' modifies سرای (a place-word). The whole segment describes motion into a specific room: 'he came into the curtained vestibule.'
segments.s667-g316: بفرمود is an honorific/past-form verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate clause expressing purpose or result 'that / so that.' What follows (s159) supplies the content of the order. سنج و هندی درای are the objects/items he ordered brought; سنج here is an item name (instrument) and هندی درای is a nominal phrase (both treated as objects of the command). The literal structure is 'he ordered that [sanj and Indian ...].'
segments.s667-g317: بیارند is a subjunctive/imperative-form verb 'they bring' (here narratively describing what was to be brought) and heads the verb phrase. با introduces accompaniment 'with'; زنده modifies پیلان, so با زنده پیلان = 'with living elephants.' و کوس joins the phrase indicating they also brought/used 'kūs' (war-drums). The clause lists what was brought and used in the procession.
segments.s667-g318: کنند is the subjunctive/optative/present-subordinate plural of کردن 'to make/cause' and here means 'they caused / made.' آسمان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the sky' as the thing affected. به رنگ آبنوس is a prepositional phrase 'in the color of ebony' functioning as result/comparison. Together: 'they made the sky (appear) in the color of ebony' (i.e., made the sky look black).
segments.s667-g319: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' این نامور جنگ is the noun phrase 'this famed/renowned war' (این = this; نامور = famed; جنگ = war/battle). را is the object marker attached to جنگ. کرد ساز is a verbal phrase: کرد (past of کردن 'made/did') plus ساز (here a noun meaning 'preparation/device' or 'making ready'), so together 'he made ready / prepared' (literally 'did preparation'). The clause as a whole: 'When he prepared this renowned battle...'.
segments.s667-g320: پر اندیشه شد is a result-state clause: پر اندیشه means 'full of thought' (پر = full (of), اندیشه = thought/meditation). شد is the past copula/auxiliary 'became.' شاه گردن‌فراز is the subject noun phrase 'the proud/lofty-necked king' (گردن‌فراز literal 'neck-raising' = proud). Word order places the resultant predicate before the subject (poetic flexibility): 'the proud king became full of thought.'
segments.s667-g169: ز is the poetic partitive/prepositional 'of/from' placed before لشکر to mark 'of the army'; بسی here functions as a quantifying adverb meaning 'many' or 'much' qualifying لشکر; نیز = 'also/too' adds inclusion; بیکار بود is the past copular predicate 'were idle' (بود = past 'was/were'). Word order is topical (prepositional/quantifier phrase) followed by the predication.
segments.s667-g170: بدان تنگی = a prepositional/demonstrative phrase meaning 'in/at that tightness/distress' (ب marking direction/relation and دان functioning as demonstrative here); اندر گرفتار بود = 'was caught/entangled within' with گرفتار (trapped) as predicate and بود as the past copula. The whole clause says they were trapped in that narrow/distressing situation.
segments.s667-g171: چو starts a simile 'like/as'; دیوار پیلان is a genitive grouping 'a wall of elephants' (دیوار = barrier, پیلان = elephants); به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'in front of the army' (به + پیش = before, سپاه = army). The image is that they formed an elephant-wall before the host.
segments.s667-g172: فراز آوریدند is a verb phrase where فراز ('on high/above') modifies آوریدند ('they brought/raised') — 'they raised (them) aloft'; و بستند راه = 'and they closed the way' with بستند (past of بستن) meaning 'shut/blocked' and راه = 'way/road'. Both verbs are past plural, narrating actions that blocked the road.
segments.s667-g173: پس 'then' introduces the result; اندر غمی شد = 'became in sorrow/was plunged into grief' with شد (become) used inchoatively; دل ساوه شاه = 'the heart of the king of Saveh' (possessive stacking: دل [of] ساوه شاه). The clause literally: 'Then the heart of the king of Saveh became in grief.'
segments.s667-g174: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'for/that'; تنگ آمدش is an idiomatic experiential construction meaning 'seemed narrow to him' or 'felt cramped to him' where آمد takes a dative clitic ش = 'to him'; جایگاه سپاه = 'the place/quarters of the army' (جایگاه = place, سپاه = army). Word order places the predicate تنگ آمدش before the noun phrase جایگاه سپاه which it describes.
segments.s667-g175: تو گفتی = 'you said' (direct address). بگرید همی is a classical verbal form: the prefix ب- with the present/subjunctive stem گرِیَد gives an optative/subjunctive sense 'may he weep' or 'let him weep'; همی adds emphasis ('ever/indeed'). بخت اوی = 'his fortune/fate' (اوی here functioning possessively 'his'). The clause reports the addressee's utterance that his fate should lament him.
segments.s667-g176: که introduces the reason/explanation 'that/for'; بیکار خواهد is a predicate with خواهد as the future auxiliary 'will be' and بیکار here meaning 'left empty/idle' (i.e., vacant); بدن تخت اوی is the noun phrase 'the body/person of his throne' (بدن = body/person; تخت = throne; اوی = his). Classical word order places the predicate before the noun phrase: 'will be vacant his throne (of a body).' The sense is 'his throne will be left vacant of him.'
segments.s667-g393: برآورد is a past-tense 3rd-person verb 'he brought/placed/put out' here meaning 'deployed/arranged'. ده رش is a numeric phrase 'ten [units]' where رش is used poetically as a small unit/row; به گل هر دو راه is a prepositional phrase 'along/to each of the two roads' with به marking direction and هر دو راه 'both roads'. Word order is Verb + object + directional phrase.
segments.s667-g394: همی‌بود is an imperfective/past-continuous form 'he remained/was (continually)'. خود follows the verb for emphasis ('he himself'). در میان سپاه is a locative phrase 'in the middle of the army/host' (در میان = 'among/in the midst of').
segments.s667-g395: This is a sequence of titles and epithets in apposition: دبیر بزرگ 'the great secretary' and جهاندار شاه 'the world‑ruling king' (جهاندار as an epithet modifying شاه). The words stand as a nominal phrase identifying a person.
segments.s667-g396: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بر here marks motion/attention 'to/toward' and governs the following nominal phrase پهلوان سپاه, where پهلوان (champion) is in a genitive relationship with سپاه (the host/army): 'to the champion of the host.'
segments.s667-g397: بدو گفت is a common sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', followed by گفت 'said' in past). کاین را is a subordinate demonstrative clause 'that this' with را marking the clause as object; خود اندازه نیست = 'is not measured/fitting in itself' — اندازه نیست means 'is not proper/within measure'.
segments.s667-g398: گزاف زبان تو را is an NP meaning 'your excessive/groundless speech' with گزاف qualifying زبان and تو را ('your') marking possession/emphasis. تازه نیست is a copular negation 'is not new' — here idiomatically 'not a new/novel claim'. Word order places modifiers before the head noun and the possessive marker after it.
segments.s667-g399: ز لشکر نگه کن is an imperative construction: ز + لشکر 'regarding the army' or 'from the army', نگه کن 'look/take heed' (نگه = gaze/watch, کن = imperative 'do'). برین رزمگاه is a locative/demonstrative phrase 'upon/over that battlefield' (بر + ین/این forming 'that' + رزمگاه 'battlefield').
segments.s667-g400: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'. موی سپیدیم is a verbless/copular description 'we are white-haired' (موی 'hair' with سپیدیم 'we are white/grey'), and و گاو سیاه adds a contrasting image 'and a black ox.' The clause compares the speakers to these images; سپیدیم is 1st‑person plural copula-form.
segments.s667-g385: خروشی (a shout/roar) is the nominal subject and برآمد is a past verb meaning 'arose/issued forth.' ز پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from before/in front of the host' (ز = poetic 'from', پیش = 'in front of'). Word order here is noun + verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s667-g386: که‌ای is a vocative formula here ('O ...') addressing the following group; گرزداران is the plural agent noun 'mace-bearers' (those who bear maces); زرین modifies کلاه and means 'golden,' so زرین کلاه = 'golden helmet(s).' The phrase is a direct vocative directed at those in front of the host.
segments.s667-g387: ز لشکر = 'from the army/host.' کسی کو ... گریزد is an archaic relative/conditional construction meaning 'whoever/anyone who flees'; here کو functions as an old particle linking the relative idea (not a locative). گریزد is present/subjunctive in form but yields the general conditional 'flees.' ز جنگ = 'from battle.'
segments.s667-g388: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' شیر پیش آیدش = 'a lion comes before him' (پیش = 'before/in front of'), with آیدش ('comes to/for him') as the verb plus enclitic object. The final گر after آیدش is a classical conditional/particle echoing the if-clause; پلنگ is the predicate noun ('(it is) a leopard'). The clause thus states the paradox that a fleeing man treats a lion as if it were only a leopard.
segments.s667-g389: به یزدان is an oath 'by God' (به used idiomatically for taking an oath). که introduces an emphatic subordinate clause ('that...'). از تن ببرم سرش literally 'I will take his head from his body'—ببرم is first-person present/subjunctive used as a vow/intention ('I will take off'), and ش is the third-person possessive clitic 'his.' The prepositional از قبل of the object ('from the body') precedes the verb for emphasis.
segments.s667-g390: به آتش بسوزم = 'I will burn (him) in the fire'; به + noun here means 'into/into the fire.' بسوزم is again first-person present/subjunctive used as a forceful intention/pledge ('I will burn'), and تن و پیکرش lists the objects 'his body and his form/figure' (پیکرش includes the possessive clitic).
segments.s667-g391: ز دو سوی لشکرش = 'from/beside the two sides of his army' — سوی here means 'side/direction.' دو راه بود = 'there were two roads/paths' with بود used as the past existential 'there were.' The phrase describes that on either side of his army two paths existed.
segments.s667-g392: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'such that/for' and بگریختن is the masdar (gerund/infinitive) 'to flee/escaping' used nominally; راه کوتاه بود = 'the way/path was short.' Together the clause means 'for the route for fleeing was short' (i.e., escape was easy/nearby).
segments.s667-g209: همان is a demonstrative before the noun meaning “that/that very.” پیل is the head noun “elephant.” بر + گستوان‌ور functions as a compound adjectival phrase literally “on/with broad/mighty shoulders” (بر here = “with/over”), and the following هزار (omitted in the provided word list) is a hyperbolic numeral modifying گستوان‌ور (“like a thousand [mighty shoulders]”). Word order (demonstrative → noun → adjectival phrase) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s667-g210: که introduces a relative clause (“which/that”). بگریزد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form (here used as a narrative/present meaning “flees” or “will flee”); classical Persian often uses the prefix بـ. از is the preposition “from.” بوی is the noun “smell/scent.” ایشان is the archaic/polite plural pronoun used here as a possessive/plural referent “their” or “of them.” سوار is the agent “rider/horseman.” The clause literally reads “that (from) whose scent the rider flees.”
segments.s667-g211: به ایران‌زمین is a locative prepositional phrase “to/into the land of Iran.” هرک is the indefinite/relative “whoever.” پیش + آیدم here forms the expression “comes before me / comes to me”: classical Persian often attaches the clitic -م to a 3rd-person verb (آید + -م) to mean “comes to/before me.” So the phrase means “whoever comes before me (in Iran).”
segments.s667-g212: ازان is a compound prepositional/demonstrative “from that / from it.” آمدن is the verbal noun “the coming / arrival.” رنج is “suffering / hardship.” نفزایدم is the verb composed of negative prefix ن + فزایدم (فزودن/فزاییدن “to increase”) with 1st-person singular ending: here it means “I will not increase / I do not add.” The whole segment reads “I will not increase the suffering from that coming.”
segments.s667-g213: از ایدر is the adverbial phrase “from here.” The following elements mark the extent/goal: تا = “as far as / until”; در طیسفون = “in/at Ctesiphon.” The intervening short forms (morphemic markers in the supplied list) reflect classical cliticization; the overall phrase gives the range “from here up to (the gate of) Ctesiphon.”
segments.s667-g214: سپاهست is a fused copular expression (سپاه است) meaning “there is an army / it is an army.” مانا is an adjective meaning “lasting / enduring” (or read as an epithet). که introduces a relative/subordinate clause, and باشد is 3rd-person subjunctive/jussive of بودن used to indicate possibility or wish (“may be / might be”). فزون is an adjective “more / abundant.” Together: “there is a lasting army, which may be more numerous.”
segments.s667-g215: تو is the vocative/address “you.” را‌ای here contains the object/definite marker را plus the vocative particle ای (rendered together in classical order as a strong address: “O ...”). بد اختر is the epithet “bad / ill-omened star.” که introduces a subordinate/relative clause. بفریفته‌ست is a perfect/resultive form (بـ + فریفتن + است) meaning “has beguiled / has deceived / has bewitched” — in context it links the addressee and the deceiver: “you, O ill-star, whom (the deceiver) has beguiled.”
segments.s667-g216: فریبنده is the agent noun “deceiver” (one who deceives). تو is the possessor/object pronoun “your / of you.” مگر here functions as a modal particle meaning “perhaps / maybe / is it that” (a rhetorical/uncertain qualifier). شیفته‌ست is the predicative “is enamored / is infatuated.” The phrase reads “your deceiver, perhaps, is enamored.”
segments.s667-g361: تو را is a topicalized/affected-object phrase ('you' with the object marker را) putting the person in focus. پیشه (profession/trade) is the predicate noun and دامست is an archaic predicate (here 'to be a trapper/one who sets snares' — a terse poetic formation). بر آبگیر is a locative prepositional phrase ('on/at the pond') modifying that predicate.
segments.s667-g362: نه negates the following noun phrase: 'not a man/hero'. مردی is an indefinite noun ('a man' / 'a warrior') and به introduces instruments or accompaniment ('with' / 'by means of'). The string گوپال و شمشیر و تیر lists weapons/tools joined by و (and); the whole phrase says he is not a man armed with these implements.
segments.s667-g363: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. خور 'the sun' is the subject. برزند is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'rise/come up' (here describing the sun's movement). سر is the direct object 'head' and ز کوه سیاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the black mountain' indicating origin.
segments.s667-g364: نمایم is 1st‑person singular (I will show / I show) used here with a future/intent sense. تو را is the object ('you' with the object marker). جنگ با ساوه شاه is the noun phrase 'the war/battle with the king of Saveh' introduced by the preposition با ('with'). The verb governs the whole following noun phrase as its object.
segments.s667-g365: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. The verb is the light-prefixed compound بر زد (بر + زد) — here a past action 'lifted/struck up' — with سر 'head' as its object. از چشمه شیر is a genitive/locative phrase 'from the spring of the lion' and شید appears as an appositive/epithet (a name or epithet meaning 'beloved'/'love‑mad') tied to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s667-g366: جهان گشت is a simple past/resultative clause: گشت 'became/turned' takes جهان 'the world' as subject. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and روی رومی سپید is the noun phrase being compared: روی 'face', رومی 'Roman', and سپید 'white' (adjective following the noun in poetic word order).
segments.s667-g367: بزد is a 3rd‑person past tense verb 'he struck'. نای رویین is a noun + adjective sequence ('the reed‑flute, iron‑coated/iron‑sounding') with the adjective following the noun in classical style. و links the clauses, and برشد خروش is an intransitive past construction ('an outcry rose/was lifted') where برشد (rose up) is the verb and خروش is the subject/what rose.
segments.s667-g368: زمین آمد uses آمد ('came') with زمین 'the earth' as subject — here idiomatically 'the earth was moved' or 'the earth surged'. از نعل اسبان marks the cause/source 'from the horses' horseshoes', and به جوش is a prepositional‑like phrase 'into boiling/surge' describing the manner ('seethed'). The whole clause describes the earth trembling/boiling from the pounding of horses.
segments.s667-g121: بگویش is the imperative بگو with the enclitic -ش (to him/her), meaning 'tell him/her'. که‌گر is an archaic conditional conjunction meaning 'if'. من چنین کهترم is a nominal clause 'I am such a lesser/lowly one' (کهترم = adjective + first-person ending), so the whole segment = 'Tell him that if I am so lowly'.
segments.s667-g122: نه here negates the predicate. ننگ آید uses آید (present/subjunctive/optative third-person of آمدن) with the noun ننگ as subject — literal 'shame comes' — so 'no shame will come'. از کهتری sets the source/cause 'from/of my being lesser' (از = 'from'), and بر سرم is a locative idiom 'upon my head' meaning 'upon me' (سرم = 'my head').
segments.s667-g123: This is a noun phrase: شهنشاه 'the king/emperor' coordinated with آن لشکر 'that army'. از ننگ تو is a prepositional phrase 'because of/for the shame of you' (از here = 'because of' / 'on account of'), which modifies the whole subject.
segments.s667-g124: به تندی is an adverbial 'with haste/ rashly'. نجوید is the present/optative third-person negative form of جستن/جست‌ن meaning here 'will not seek/pursue' or 'does not desire'; همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle (ongoing or emphatic aspect). جنگ تو is the object 'your fight/battle'. Word order presents adverb — verb — aspect particle — object.
segments.s667-g125: من is the subject 'I'. از خردگی is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/for lowliness (humiliation)'. رانده‌ام is the perfect/compound form with the first-person suffix 'I have been driven' or 'I have driven' (rān+ide auxiliary pattern; here context implies 'I have been driven/compelled'), and با سپاه 'with an army' is an instrumental/associative phrase describing accompaniment.
segments.s667-g126: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. ویران کنم is a present-subjunctive/intentional construction 'I may destroy / I will lay waste' (ویران = ruin; کنَم = 'I do/make' used as the personal verb). لشکر ساوه شاه is a genitive chain 'the host/army of the king of Saveh' (lshkr = object; ساوه شاه = 'Saveh's king').
segments.s667-g127: There are two coordinated actions expressed by identical-root verbs: ببرم (from بردن) 'I will take/carry' with object سرت را 'your head' marked by the object marker را; the second برم is a separate verb clause 'I will carry/bring' governed by نزد شاه 'to the presence of the king'. The sequence reads as two coordinated actions: 'I will take your head, I will bring it to the king.'
segments.s667-g128: نیرزد is a fixed impersonal/predicate meaning 'it is not worth/it does not merit'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'. بر نیزه سازم combines the prepositional phrase بر نیزه 'upon a spear' with سازم (from ساختن) 'I should make/put' — here 'I should set (it) on a spear'. به راه is a locative 'on the road' or 'along the way', giving the place where setting on a spear would occur.
segments.s667-g305: زمان is the noun 'time' functioning as the direct object; داده‌ام is the compound past/present-perfect 1st-person singular 'I have given' (داد + -ه‌ام). شاه را is the recipient marked by the object/definite marker را (classical Persian often omits an explicit preposition for the indirect object). تا سه روز is a duration/limit phrase 'up to/for three days' (تا here = 'up to').
segments.s667-g306: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that'); پیدا شود is a two-word predication where پیدا = 'become apparent/appear' and شود is the present-subjunctive/auxiliary 'may become' giving the sense 'may appear'. فر گیتی فروز is a poetic nominal phrase: فر 'royal glory' qualified by گیتی فروز 'world-illuminating' (گیتی + فروز functioning as an epithet).
segments.s667-g307: بریده سرت را is a noun phrase 'your severed head' where بریده is a participial/adjectival form 'cut off/severed', سرت = 'your head' (سر + enclitic -ت), and را marks the definite object. بدان بارگاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at that court' — here the classical prefix ب(=به) + an archaic/demonstrative form (presented as دان in the tokenization) combine with بارگاه; functionally this is 'به آن بارگاه' ('to/at that palace').
segments.s667-g308: ببینند is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive used here as an optative/imperative-like 'let them see' or narrative future 'they will see'. بر نیزه is a locative phrase 'on a spear' where بر = 'on/upon' and نیزه = 'spear/pike'. در پیش شاه = 'in the presence of the king' (در + پیش giving the locative 'before/in front of the king').
segments.s667-g309: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' (subject). آمد is simple past 'came'. دو رخ is a descriptive epithet 'two-faced' / 'two-cheeked' placed after the verb; چون + زریر introduces a simile 'like Zarir' (چون = 'like'). The clause reads 'a messenger came, (with) face like Zarir's.'
segments.s667-g310: شده بارور is a perfect/periphrastic expression 'has become fruitful/prosperous' (شده = 'become', بارور = 'fruitful/prosperous'). بخت = 'fortune' or 'fate' and برناش is the adjective برنا ('youthful, vigorous') with a possessive/relational ending (-ش) in the manuscript form; پیر = 'old' functions as a contrasting predicate. The sequence expresses a paradoxical state: fortune has made him prosperous yet he is old (literally 'fortune [for] his youth — old').
segments.s667-g311: همی‌داد is an iterative/imperfective past 'kept giving' / 'kept sending' (همی‑prefix marks ongoing or repeated action). پیغام is the direct object 'a message'. با ساوه شاه is a prepositional phrase identifying the addressee/associate — 'with/ to the king of Saveh' (با here indicates association/recipient with the city-name ساوه specifying which king).
segments.s667-g312: چو = 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (3rd sing.); شد is the verb 'became' (perfective/resultative). روی مهتر سیاه is a nominal predicate: روی (face) + مهتر (the elder/chief, here the possessor or subject) + سیاه (black/dark) — word order places the possessor/subject (مهتر) after روی and the predicative adjective (سیاه) last: 'the elder's face became dark.'
segments.s667-g137: بیامد بگفت is two past verbs in sequence: بیامد (literally “he came”) + بگفت (honorific/past “he said/reported”), used here as a simple narrative 'he came and said'. آنچ is a relative/indefinite pronoun meaning “that which” and takes the two following past verbs (دید و شنید) as its complements: ‘that which he saw and heard’. The verbs دید و شنید are perfective past forms; word order places the relative (آنچ) before the verbs for emphasis.
segments.s667-g138: This clause gives the reaction: سر here means 'head' in the figurative sense of the ruler/leader (the chief), followed by شاه ترکان 'the king of the Turks'. ز introduces the cause/reason 'because of/with', and کین is 'rage/vengeance'. بردمید is an archaic past form meaning 'was seized' / 'was taken' (here best understood as 'was seized by' — i.e. 'was overcome by rage'). The construction thus reads: 'the Turkish king was seized with/overcome by rage.'
segments.s667-g139: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past used as 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that', which governs the subjunctive/indexed verb برند (third person plural subjunctive: 'they bring/carry'), giving an instruction. کوس ('war-drum') is the direct object and بیرون ('out') marks the direction/place: 'bring the drums out.'
segments.s667-g140: سرافراز is an adjective modifying پیلان (elephants): 'triumphant/proud elephants.' The preposition به + هامون gives direction/place 'onto/into the plain.' برند repeats the subjunctive plural verb from the previous clause and so coordinates the two commands: 'let the elephants be brought onto the plain.' Word order (adjective before noun, prepositional phrase after) is typical of classical/Persian poetry.
segments.s667-g141: سیه شد is a copular/perfective expression 'became dark/black'. همه کشور is the subject 'the whole country/land'; the postpositional phrase از گرد سم gives the cause or source: از ('from') + گرد ('dust') + سم ('hoof'), literally 'from the dust of hooves.' The phrase describes the land darkened by hoof-dust raised by many animals.
segments.s667-g142: برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/issued forth'. It takes the verbal noun/infinitive خروشیدن ('to roar' / 'a roaring') as its logical subject: 'a roaring arose.' گاودم is poetic and here accompanies خروشیدن to specify the source of the sound (literally 'cow-tail' / 'the oxen'), so the whole phrase means 'the roar/bellow of oxen rose.' The compact sequence is typical of epic diction: verb (برآمد) + verbal noun + source.
segments.s667-g143: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the past 'he heard'; what follows, کآمد سپاه, is the content of that hearing: کآمد (past 'came/arrived') + سپاه (army) — a subordinate clause with inverted order (verb before subject), a common poetic word order: 'when he heard (that) the army had come.'
segments.s667-g144: در و دشت is a paired locative phrase 'town and plain' (در = in/town; دشت = plain). شد is 'became' and is followed by three color/adjective terms joined by و: سرخ (red), زرد (yellow/pale), سیاه (black). The sequence gives a vivid, compressed image: both city and plain were transformed into these colors — a poetic way to evoke banners, blood, dust, etc.
segments.s667-g145: سپه را is the direct object (سپه 'army' with the object marker را). بفرمود is the past, honorific/courtly verb 'he ordered/commanded/said' (third person). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that') and is followed by برنشست, a past-tense verb 'took a seat/sat (upon)' describing the action ordered.
segments.s667-g146: بیامد is a simple past 'came' (third person). زره دار is a compound noun in which زره 'armor' combines with دار (here functioning as '-bearer'/'holder') to mean 'armour-bearer'; و links the next descriptor; گهرزی is an adjective/epithet 'jewel‑adorned' used as a noun ('the jewel‑bedecked one'); به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in (his/their) hand' or 'holding (it) in hand' and attaches to the coming forward (بیامد).
segments.s667-g147: پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. پشت here means 'rear/behind' and takes the following noun شارستان هری 'the town/city of Hari' in a genitive relation (the rear of the town of Hari). بد is an attributive adjective 'bad/ill' modifying پشت, giving a sense like 'the poorly‑defended/ill‑set rear of the town of Hari.'
segments.s667-g148: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'toward the front / to the fore'. اندرون 'inside/within' narrows the location to the inner part. تیغ‌زن لشکری is a compound: تیغ‌زن 'sword‑striker, swordsman' + لشکری 'military / troop', together meaning 'a troop/contingent of sword‑wielding soldiers.' The phrase describes who was placed toward the front and inside.
segments.s667-g149: بیاراست is a past transitive 'he arranged/arrayed' (here about arranging troops). با is the preposition 'with' used to mark the two flanks. میمنه and میسره are the classical pair meaning 'right (flank)' and 'left (flank)' respectively, so با میمنه میسره means 'with right and left' i.e. 'on the right and on the left.'
segments.s667-g150: سپاهی is the noun 'soldiers / troops'. همه is a quantifier 'all, every one'. کینه کش is a compound in which کینه 'hatred/grudge' combines with کش (an agentive element) to mean 'those who carry out/are driven by hatred' — effectively 'grudge‑bearers / vengeance‑seekers'. یکسره is an adverb 'completely / utterly' (here emphasizing their single‑minded resolve).
segments.s667-g151: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. گفتی is the simple past 'you said' (here introducing reported speech). جهان یکسر از آهنست is a predicate clause: جهان 'the world' + یکسر 'entirely' (adverb) + از آهنست 'is of iron' (آهنست = 'is of iron' / 'made of iron'), an attributive/metaphorical statement about the world.
segments.s667-g152: ستاره is 'star' (subject). ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از meaning 'from'. نوک سنان is a genitive phrase 'the tips of spears' (نوک 'tip' + سنان 'spears'). روشنست is the predicate 'is bright/clear' (archaic/poetic third‑person form 'is bright'), saying the stars gleam because of/above the spear‑tips.
segments.s667-g425: This is a verbless nominal clause describing what the addressee perceives. گرین (an adjective) modifies جنگ; بیداد is a second modifier giving the sense of cruelty/oppression. بینی is the 2nd-person present singular of 'to see' (you see/behold) placed after its nominal phrase, and همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker attached to the verb, giving a present/ongoing sense.
segments.s667-g426: ز + من = 'from me / of me' (ز is the poetic preposition). ساوه را is the definite object (را marks Saveh as the thing affected). برگزینی is 2nd-person singular present 'you choose/select' (here rendered as 'you take/choose'), and همی again marks present/continuous aspect — so 'you are taking/you take'.
segments.s667-g427: دلم را is the direct object marked by را ('my heart'). به رزم اندر is an adverbial phrase 'in/amid battle' (به + رزم + اندر 'within'). آرام ده is an imperative: آرام (peace/calm) as the thing to be given and ده (2nd-person singular imperative of دادن) 'give'. The clause thus means 'give my heart calm/peace amid battle.'
segments.s667-g428: به ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase marking the target/beneficiary ('to/toward the Iranians'). بر here functions as a lexical particle meaning 'upon/against/toward' that connects to the following imperative. The comma separates clauses; ورا کام ده is another imperative: ورا = 'to him/thereupon' (third‑person object), کام ده = 'grant (his) desire' (کام = desire, ده = give).
segments.s667-g429: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). من is the subject 'I'; ز بهر تو is a purpose phrase 'for your sake' (ز = from/for, بهر = for, تو = you). کوشم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of کوشیدن 'I strive / I try', and همی is the classical continuous/habitual marker attached to the verb, giving an ongoing or emphatic present sense.
segments.s667-g430: به رزم اندرون is an adverbial phrase 'within the battle' (به + رزم + اندرون). سر فروشم is an idiomatic object + verb: سر 'head' is the thing offered/sold, فروشم (here 1st-person singular of فروختن) = 'I would sell / I will sell' (i.e. risk/sacrifice my head). همی again marks the ongoing/presentive aspect. The verb follows its object patternally.
segments.s667-g431: مرا (me) and سپاه مرا (my army) are two coordinated objects marked for the imperative. شاد کن is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'make (happy/joyful)' (شاد = happy, کن = 'do' used as auxiliary imperative). Word order places the objects before the imperative verb, a common classical pattern.
segments.s667-g432: وزین modifies جنگ ('by/with this weighty or mighty battle'); ما is the possessive 'our' qualifying جنگ. گیتی آباد کن is a second imperative clause: گیتی = the world, آباد = prosperous/flourishing (adjective used predicatively), and کن is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'make'. The whole clause is an imperative: 'by this mighty battle, make the world prosper.'
segments.s667-g17: Two coordinated interrogative nominal clauses: 'سپهبد کدامست' and 'سالار کیست' both use the copula 'ست/کیست' ('is') placed after the question word. 'سپهبد' and 'سالار' are titles; the question words 'کدام' and 'کی' follow the noun in this poetic inverted order (noun + question), so literally ‘Spahbed which-is? and commander who-is?’. The conjunction 'و' simply links the two questions.
segments.s667-g18: 'به رزم اندرون' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/within the battle' (به = toward/into; اندرون = inside). 'نامبردار' is an epithet 'the famed/renowned' modifying an implied person; 'کیست' is again the copula-question 'who is (it)?'. Word order is verb-final-questionless style common in Persian poetry: the locative ('به رزم اندرون') precedes the predicate ('نامبردار کیست').
segments.s667-g19: This clause begins with the subordinating/relative particle 'که' ('that/when/for'), introducing the circumstance. 'فغفور' is the title (Byzantine emperor). The phrase 'چشم و دل' pairs two nouns ('eye and heart') as a single figurative object meaning both his gaze and affection (i.e. he set his sight and desire). 'ساوه شاه' is a genitival phrase 'the king of Saveh' where the place-name precedes 'شاه' in classical order.
segments.s667-g20: 'ورا' = 'to him/at him' (direct object reference). 'دید' is past 'saw' or 'set (one's eyes on)'; here it combines with 'چشم و دل' to mean he fixed his look and heart on that king. 'خواهد' is the present/future auxiliary meaning 'he desires/wishes' (classical Persian often stacks aspect/time forms). 'همی' is an emphatic/old poetic particle ('indeed/still'); and 'بی‌سپاه' is an adjectival phrase 'without an army' describing the desired state. Syntactically: ‘he saw/fixed his heart on him and desires him unescorted.’
segments.s667-g21: 'ز لشکر بیامد' is a fronted prepositional phrase 'from the army came' (poetic inversion: prepositional phrase before the verb). 'یکی رزمجوی' is the subject 'a certain warrior' (یکی here = 'a certain/someone'), placed after the verb for stylistic effect: 'from the army came a warrior.'
segments.s667-g22: 'به بهرام گفت' = 'he said to Bahram' (به + name = to Bahram; گفت functions as the speech verb). 'آنچ' is the archaic form of 'آن‌چه' ('that which/what'), and 'بشنید' is past 'he had heard'. 'زوی' = 'from him' (source marker). Together: 'he told Bahram that which he had heard from him.' Note classical sequence: main verb 'گفت' governs the content clause introduced by 'آنچ' + past verb 'بشنید.'
segments.s667-g23: 'سپهدار آمد' is simple past narrative 'the commander came.' 'ز پرده سرای' is a locative/origin phrase 'from behind the curtain of the chamber/pavilion' (ز = from). Note that 'سرای' in classical usage normally means 'house/room/pavilion' (here 'the curtained room'), though the provided sense list does not include that meaning.
segments.s667-g24: 'درفشی درفشان' is a descriptive pair: 'درفشی' (relating to the banner/standard) and 'درفشان' ('shining, glittering' or 'banner-bearing'); together they describe the figure as a shining standard-bearer. The repeated prepositional sequence 'به سر بر به پای' = 'on the head and on the foot' (literally 'to head upon to foot') where each 'به' governs a following noun; this poetic double preposition emphasizes that the standard is displayed from head to foot (i.e. conspicuously).
segments.s667-g89: ور is a conditional 'if'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'come' (here used in a protasis: 'if ever you come'). که introduces the subordinate clause. ایدر (here) follows the clause-marker and the preposition به + جنگ 'into/for battle' places the purpose/direction before the verb. آمدی is the second-person past-form used in classical Persian to express a hypothetical or eventual action ('you come' / 'you should come').
segments.s667-g90: A parallel clause repeating the conditional idea: به دریا 'to/into the sea' (preposition + noun) sets the location, then another به + جنگ phrase marks the goal 'to fight'. نهنگ is the object 'whale'. آمدی again is the same second-person past-form used for the hypothetical 'you come/should come'.
segments.s667-g91: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb. بازگردی is the verbal form 'you return' (classical present/subjunctive form used to indicate future/habitual action). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دشت هری is a genitive/place phrase 'the plain of Harī'. Word order places the adverb and verb before the source phrase.
segments.s667-g92: که introduces a result/clause of consequence here ('so that'). بر تو is the preposition بر + object pronoun 'upon you' (meaning 'for you' / 'over you'). بگریند is plural 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'they weep' (classical form with ب-). هر مهتری is the subject phrase 'every chief/notable' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s667-g93: به here functions as a locative/prepositional marker 'on/at' (on your return). برگشتنت is the noun/gerund 'your return' formed from برگشتن + possessive suffix -ت. پیش 'before' modifies the following place phrase در چاه 'in/at the well'. باد at the end here is the optative particle ('may there be') as well as literally 'wind' — context shows the optative sense.
segments.s667-g94: Two short optative clauses joined by و. پست باد = 'may (the) wind be low' (پست = 'low', باد = optative 'may it be' or 'wind' used optatively). و = 'and'. بارانت is 'your rain' (باران + possessive -ت). همراه باد = 'may it be accompanied' / 'may (it) be together' with همراه 'together/with' and the final باد again functioning as the optative particle 'may'. The repetition of باد as optative is a classical poetic device.
segments.s667-g95: نیاوردت is the negative past verb 'did not bring' with the clitic object -ت 'you' ('did not bring you' / 'nothing brought you'). ایدر 'here' locates the statement. مگر = 'except' (introducing the exception). بخت بد = 'bad fortune' (بخت = fate/fortune, بد = bad). The implied subject ('anything' / 'anything beneficial') is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s667-g96: همی is an adverb 'continually/always'. خواست is the past tense 'wished/wanted' (third person) whose subject is carried over from the previous clause (بخت بد). تا introduces a final/subjunctive clause meaning 'that/so that'. بر سرت = 'upon your head' (بر + سرت). بد رسد uses بد 'bad' as the thing wished and رسد in the subjunctive/optative sense 'may it come / befall' (here 'befall' is the intended sense).
segments.s667-g377: "به دست چپش بود پیدا گشسب" is a locative + predicate phrase: به + noun phrase (به دست چپش = 'at/on his left hand') comes first, then the copula بود ('was') at the end, with پید ا ('visible, apparent') modifying the following name گشسب. Word order is typical Persian SVO/Suffix-order for possession/locatives where the verb 'بود' closes the clause.
segments.s667-g378: The relative clause introduced by که ('who/that') describes Geshsep. بگذاشتی here is a past action ('placed/put' or 'had poured'); آب دریا ('water of the sea') is the object and براسب ('upon the horse' / 'on horseback') is a locative adverbial placed at the end. The clause therefore reads 'who had placed/poured the sea's water upon the horse.'
segments.s667-g379: پس ('then/therefore') opens the clause. پشت ایشان ('behind them') is the locative expression, followed by the subject یلان ('heroes'); سینه here qualifies those heroes (broad‑chested / stout‑chested). The copula بود ('were') closes the clause, so literal order is 'then behind them heroes breast was' -> 'then behind them were broad‑chested heroes.'
segments.s667-g380: که ('who') begins a relative clause describing those heroes. با ('with') introduces the instruments: جوشن ('cuirass/armor') and گرز ('mace'), joined by و ('and'); دیرینه ('ancient/long‑standing') modifies گرز. The clause ends with بود ('were'), so 'who were with armor and ancient maces.'
segments.s667-g381: به پیش اندرون ('at the front inside') is a compound locative adjunct (به + پیش + اندرون) placed before the verb; همدان گشسب is the proper name ('Geshsep of Hamadan') as the subject, and بود ('was') is the copula placed before the name in this poetic inversion. The overall sense is 'at the fore, within, was Hamadan's Geshsep.'
segments.s667-g382: که ('who') introduces a relative clause. در نی is instrumental/locative phrasing using نی ('ney', reed‑flute) and در ('with/in'); زدی is the past tense of زدن ('struck' / 'caused'), آتش ('fire') is the affected result and از سم اسب ('from the horse's hoof') gives the source. The clause poetically says he 'struck/fire issued from the horse's hoof with the reed.'
segments.s667-g383: ابا هر یکی سه هزار از یلان is a distributive phrase: هر یکی ('each one') governs the quantity سه هزار ('three thousand') which applies to از یلان ('of the heroes'). ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic particle introducing the enumeration ('there were' / 'each had'). The clause means 'each had three thousand of the heroes.'
segments.s667-g384: This is a nominal list describing those troops: سواران ('horsemen/riders') modified by جنگی ('warlike, military'), followed by و ('and') and a compound جنگ آوران ('war‑bringers' = valiant fighters). The sequence enumerates types of fighters — 'horsemen, warlike (fighters) and bringers of battle.'
segments.s667-g153: نگه کرد is a periphrastic past: نگه (gaze) + کرد (did) = 'looked'. زان is an archaic/poetic form of آن meaning 'that'. رزمگاه ساوه شاه is a genitive construction in which ساوه (Saveh) and شاه (king) together indicate 'the king of Saveh' or 'the Saveh-king's battlefield' (classical Persian allows flexible word order), so the clause literally reads 'he made a gaze at that battlefield of the king of Saveh.'
segments.s667-g154: به here is the preposition introducing the thing looked at (literally 'to/at'); آرایش و ساز is a coordinated noun phrase ('array/arrangement and equipment/means'), and آن رزمگاه repeats/specifies 'that battlefield' (آن = 'that', followed by رزمگاه). The whole segment qualifies what he looked at: 'at the array and accoutrements of that battlefield.'
segments.s667-g155: هری is the place-name (Hari/Harirud or region). از پس پشت is a stacked prepositional phrase meaning 'from behind' or simply 'behind' (از + پس + پشت = literary emphasis on rear). بهرام بود places the existence/location in the past ('Bahram had/was [the Hari] behind him' → idiomatically 'Behind Bahram lay the Hari').
segments.s667-g156: همه جای خود is a possessive phrase meaning 'all his places/positions' or 'everywhere he stood'; خود here reinforces possession ('his own'). تنگ و ناکام بود is the predicate in the past tense: تنگ 'narrow, cramped', ناکام 'unsuccessful/thwarted', بود 'was'. Together: 'everywhere his position was cramped and thwarted.'
segments.s667-g157: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = thus). پس here functions adverbially as 'then' or 'thereupon'. باسواران خویش is the indirect object 'to his horsemen' (باسواران = horsemen/riders; خویش is completed by the clitic ش in the next token to mean 'his'). The clause introduces reported speech addressed to his mounted followers.
segments.s667-g158: This segment lists the kinds of men he addresses: جهاندیده 'worldly-wise/experienced' and غمگساران 'those who share or bear his sorrow' (a plural noun). خویش + ش again marks possession ('his worldly-wise and his fellow‑mourners'). It's a nominal apposition describing the audience of his speech.
segments.s667-g159: که introduces a subordinate/content clause ('that' / 'for that'). آمد is simple past 'came'. فریبنده‌ای is an indefinite noun formed with the -ای suffix meaning 'a deceiver' or 'one who deceives' (the -ای signals indefiniteness). نزد من means 'to/near me' or 'in my presence' (نزد = at/near, من = me).
segments.s667-g160: ازان is a contracted/poetic form of از آن 'from that' or 'of that'. پارسی here means 'a Persian (person)' rather than the language (sense matches 'Persian'). مهتر انجمن is a title-like noun phrase 'the chief/leader of the assembly' (classical word order allows the head and title to appear adjacent without an explicit ezāfe); together the phrase means 'from that Persian, the chief of the assembly.'
segments.s667-g41: شنید is a past-tense verb 'he heard' with no explicit subject (the subject will be the king). آن سخن is the direct object 'that speech.' شاه شد بدگمان is a linked clause where شاه is the subject and شد (past of شدن) + بدگمان is the predicate 'became suspicious.' The construction places the verb شنید before the object and then gives the separate result-clause about the king.
segments.s667-g42: فرستاده را has the object-marking را attached to 'the messenger.' جست is the past of جستن here meaning 'sought' (the king is the implied subject carried over). هم در زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at once / at the same time' (هم intensifies/ties the action to the previous moment). Word order is object + verb (typical in this poetic past narrative with omitted pronoun subject).
segments.s667-g43: یکی گفت is the idiomatic 'someone said' (یکی = 'someone/a certain one', گفت = 'said') introducing reported speech. خراد برزین forms the agent/subject of گریخت (a name or name phrase), and گریخت is the past 'fled.' The clause reports what was said; names/agents are juxtaposed before the verb.
segments.s667-g44: همی marks a durative or ongoing action ('kept on / was continually'); ز آمدن خون ('from the coming/flowing of blood') uses ز as the poetic preposition 'from' and آمدن as a noun/infinitive 'the coming' or 'flow.' ز مژگان بریخت is literal 'poured from the eyelashes' where بریخت is the past of ریختن 'poured/flowed.' The subject is implied (the person just named); the repeated ز is a poetic emphasis ('from ... from ...').
segments.s667-g45: چنین گفت پس is 'thus he said then' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'he said', پس = 'then/afterwards'). با + noun here indicates the addressee or companion: با پسر ساوه شاه = 'to/with the son of the king of Saveh.' The noun sequence پسر ساوه شاه is a genitive chain meaning 'the son of the king of Saveh.'
segments.s667-g46: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'when'). این بدگمان مرد = 'this suspicious man' (demonstrative + adjective + noun). چون here functions temporally ('when') and یافت is the past of یافتن 'found,' with راه 'a way/path' as the object; together چون یافت راه = 'when he found a way.' The clause is thus a subordinate temporal clause dependent on the preceding speech-verb.
segments.s667-g47: This is a coordinated noun phrase: شب تیره 'dark night' and لشکری بی‌شمار 'an army without number' (لشکری = 'troops, soldiery', بی‌شمار = 'countless'). The phrase sets the scene or circumstance; adjectives follow their nouns in this poetic order.
segments.s667-g48: طلایه 'the vanguard' is the topic; چرا is the interrogative 'why' placed before the verb. شد is the past of شدن 'became,' and چنین سست و خوار is the predicate 'so weak and humbled/despised' (چنین = 'so/thus', سست = 'weak', خوار = 'despised/humbled'). The line poses a rhetorical question about the vanguard's condition.
segments.s667-g97: فرستاده is the subject ('the messenger'). برگشت و آمد are two past-tense verbs in sequence ('returned and came/rushed'); poetic word order places the verbs after the subject. چو باد is a simile ('like the wind') with چو = 'like/as' modifying آمد; آمد is simple past (he came).
segments.s667-g98: پیام is the noun 'message/tidings' and جهان جوی is a compound modifier: جهان ('world') + جوی in the sense 'seeking/searching' (i.e., 'world-seeking' or that which goes to the world). یک‌یک is an adverbial 'one by one' or 'each separately'. بداد is the past verb 'gave/delivered' (poetic past form); it governs پیام as its object.
segments.s667-g99: چو is a temporal marker 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (3rd singular). پیغام او is the object phrase 'his message' (پیغام = message; او = his/he). ساوه شاه ('the king of Saveh') appears after the clause for emphasis — poetic inversion places the named subject after the verb phrase.
segments.s667-g100: برآشفت is a past/result verb meaning 'became angry/agitated'. زان is a contracted poetic form 'from that / because of that', linking the anger to what was heard. نامور رزمخواه is an epithet: نامور 'renowned' modifying رزمخواه 'war‑seeker/aspiring fighter'.
segments.s667-g101: ازان = 'from that / because of that'. سرد گفتن is a nominalized phrase meaning 'cold/stern speaking' or 'that stern utterance' (سرد = stern; گفتن used as 'utterance/saying'). دلش = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic pronoun ش = his). تنگ شد is a compound past meaning 'became tight/constricted' i.e. anxious or troubled.
segments.s667-g102: رخانش is a poetic word for his visage/countenance (رخان + enclitic ش = his), acting as the subject. ز اندیشه = 'from/because of thought' (ز = from/because of; اندیشه = thought). بی‌رنگ شد is 'became colorless/pale' (بی‌رنگ = colorless, شد = became).
