segments.s667-g103: فرستاده را uses را as the object/indirect-object marker pointing to the person being addressed ('to the messenger'). گفت is the past tense 'he said' (introducing reported speech). رو باز گرد is an imperative sequence: رو ('face/toward' = 'turn [your face]') and باز گرد ('return/turn back') — a standard poetic imperative telling someone to go back.
segments.s667-g104: پیامی is the indefinite object 'a message' (پیامی = a message). ببر is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'take!'. نزد + آن + دیومرد is the destination phrase: نزد = 'to / in the presence of', آن = 'that', دیومرد = 'demon‑man' or ferocious warrior; together 'to that demon‑man.'
segments.s667-g369: سپه را بیاراست و خود برنشست: سپه (subject/accusative) + را marks the army as a definite/affected object; بیاراست is a past tense verb meaning 'arrayed/arranged' (here transitive, literary past). و links the two clauses; خود is emphatic 'himself' (he personally), and برنشست is a past tense verb 'sat upon/took his seat' with the prefix بر- emphasizing 'on/above' + نشست (sat). The sequence is typical classical Persian V(پِ و) V: he arranged the army, and he himself sat.
segments.s667-g370: یکی گرز پرخاش دیده به دست: یکی 'one' indicates single/one hand; گرز is the concrete noun 'mace/club'; پرخاش دیده is a compound participial description: پرخاش (aggression/hostility) + دیده (past participle 'seen/appearing'), together 'appearing aggressive' or 'menacing in appearance'; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in/at the hand' specifying where the mace was held. Word order places the descriptive phrase after گرز, as is common.
segments.s667-g371: شمردند بر میمنه سه هزار: شمردند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they counted/reckoned'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' marking location; میمنه here means 'the right side/wing' (military right). The numeral phrase سه هزار follows and is the object of شمردند (they counted/numbered three thousand on the right).
segments.s667-g372: زره دار و کارآزموده سوار: زره + دار function together as 'armored' (زره 'armor'); دار here is a bound/derivational element meaning 'having' (not well captured by the senses list), forming 'زره‌دار' = 'armored'. و is the conjunction 'and'; کارآزموده is a compound adjective 'experienced/seasoned' and سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman' that both adjectives modify (armored and experienced horsemen).
segments.s667-g373: فرستاده بر میسره همچنین: فرستاده is used as a past participle/adjective 'posted/sent' (here 'posted/placed'); بر is the locative 'on/upon'; میسره is the counterpart of میمنه (meaning the left side/wing) — the sense list doesn't show that but here it is locative 'left side'; همچنین means 'likewise/also', linking the action to the previous placement on the right. Word order: participle + location phrase + adverb.
segments.s667-g374: سواران جنگی و مردان کین: سواران is the plural 'horsemen/riders'; جنگی is an adjective 'warlike/military' modifying them; و is 'and'; مردان is 'men' and کین qualifies them as 'men of vengeance' or 'vengeful men' (کین = vengeance/hatred). The two coordinated noun phrases mirror the right wing list.
segments.s667-g375: به یک دست بر بود آذر گشسب: به یک دست is a locative phrase 'in/at one hand'; بر بود is an archaic/literary combination where بر is directional/locative 'on/upon' and بود is the past copula 'was' (so 'there was upon one hand'); آذر here functions as part of the proper name 'آذر گشسب' (Azar‑Gushasp) rather than the common noun 'fire'. Word order places the locative phrase before the copula and the named entity after it.
segments.s667-g376: پرستنده فرخ ایزد گشسب: پرستنده is a participial adjective 'worshipper/devotee' modifying the following noun; فرخ ایزد is a genitival/epithet 'fortunate (فرخ) god (ایزد)' — here both are attributive; گشسب is the personal name being described. The phrase reads 'Gushasp, worshipper of the fortunate god' (the dependent genitive relation is implied).
segments.s667-g201: This clause is an older, compact Persian clause saying that nothing takes the speaker's treasure from its place. نبردارد is an archaic negative/factual predicate here (classical third‑person form), followed by the prepositional phrase از جای (“from its place”). گنج + م + را is the older word order for “my treasure”: گنج (noun) then the enclitic/old marker م indicating possession or existence (see note) and را as the definite/object marker. The whole phrase functions as the first half of a two-part assertion about what the speaker possesses and what cannot be taken.
segments.s667-g202: This is an asyndetic list of two further possessions/conditions: سلیح مرا (‘my weapon’) and ساز رنج مرا (‘(the) instrument/means of my hardship’ or more simply ‘my hardship’). Here سلیح and رنج are nouns, each followed by the same enclitic/old marker م plus را; ساز is used as a noun meaning “instrument/means” (not the musical sense), so the phrase strings together possessions/attributes of the speaker. The clause is elliptical (the verb from the previous clause is carried over).
segments.s667-g203: جز از ... در جهان is a restricting prepositional phrase: جز از “except from/of” followed by پارسی (a Persian person) and مهترت (an older comparative/epithet meaning a more eminent/greater one), then در جهان “in the world.” The order is typical classical Persian (exception phrase + noun + qualifying epithet + locative).
segments.s667-g204: This clause states the result: مرا شاه خوانند — literally ‘they call me king.’ م here is the older/existential/possessive marker placed before را, and خوانند is third‑person plural present subjunctive/indicative used as a generic ‘they call’ (classical plural form). فرخ مهان follows as a title or epithet applied to the speaker (two nouns in apposition), i.e. they call me “Farrokh‑Mahān.”
segments.s667-g205: تو را is the topicalized/accusative form ‘you’ (تو + را). هم is an adverb ‘also/too.’ زمانه here means ‘fortune/destiny’ and is the grammatical subject of the predicate that follows. به دست منست is an archaic locative/predicative phrase: به دست (‘in the hand/power of’) + منست, an older third‑person singular copular/predicative form meaning ‘is with me / lies with me.’ The clause therefore means ‘your fate is also in my hand.’
segments.s667-g206: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘before/in the presence of’ or ‘previously’; روان من here is an older expression (روان used as a nominal/relational word tied to ‘me’ — roughly ‘my presence/my going’). این روشنست is a simple predication: این ‘this’ + روشنست (archaic third‑person ‘is clear/manifest’). Together the clause says that the preceding fact ‘is clear to me beforehand’ or ‘is manifest in my presence.’
segments.s667-g207: This is a protasis (conditional clause) beginning with اگر ‘if.’ من is the subject ‘I.’ ز جای اندر آرم سپاه is an older word order meaning ‘I set the army in motion from its place’ (ز جای = from its post; اندر = in/into; آرم is the first‑person present/subjunctive/optative ‘I move/set’ in classical diction; سپاه = army/host). The clause expresses a hypothetical: ‘If I move the army from its post…’
segments.s667-g208: ببندند is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive form often used in consequence clauses to mean ‘they would shut/bind/close.’ بر introduces the objects: بر مور و بر پشه راه literally ‘upon/against the ant and against the gnat the way’ — idiomatically ‘they would block the road even to ants and gnats.’ راه is the noun ‘road/path.’ The construction is a classical way to say the road is tightly shut so that even the smallest creatures cannot pass.
segments.s667-g9: This is an adverbial span marking origin and extent: ز (poetic 'from') + پیش پدر ('before/in the presence of his father') gives the starting point; تا ('until') links that start to the following location phrase در پهلوان ('into/among the champions'). Word order is literary/inverted: the whole spatial extent phrase comes before the verb in the next segment.
segments.s667-g10: بیامد is a 3rd-person past tense verb ('there came') placed before its noun phrase in poetic inversion. خردمند and جوان are adjectival modifiers framing مرد: here the adjectives appear in front of (خردمند) and after (جوان) the noun in a literary ordering, so 'خردمند مرد جوان' reads as 'a wise young man.'
segments.s667-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آمد is the 3rd-person past ('came'). The prepositional phrase به نزدیک ایران ('to/near Iran') follows the verb, and سپاه ('army') is the subject placed after this phrase because of poetic inversion — literally 'when came to near Iran the army.'
segments.s667-g12: This is an inverted clause where the object/thing (سواری, 'riders/cavalry') appears before the verb برافگند ('sent forth/let loose' — poetic past), and فرزند شاه ('the king's son') is the subject that follows the verb. Such post-verbal subject placement is common in literary Persian.
segments.s667-g13: The initial که links to the previous narrative ('so'/'that'); پرسد is the narrative present/past 'he asked.' The second که introduces the reported question 'who' and کیند is the archaic 3rd-person plural copula ('they are'). Word order is the interrogative 'این جنگجویان کیند' = 'who are these warriors?'.
segments.s667-g14: ازین is the compound 'از' + old orthographic 'ین' = 'from this'; تاختن is a verbal noun 'the charging/attack'; ساخته is a passive/participial or resultative sense 'made/done/achieved'; بر is the preposition 'by/through/against'; چیند (from چیدن) here functions rhetorically as 'what do they gain/achieve?' — the whole line asks rhetorically what can be accomplished by this charge.
segments.s667-g15: ز ترکان سواری is a prepositional phrase 'riders from the Turks' with سواری as a collective noun; بیامد is the past verb 'came.' The conjunction چو ('when') then introduces the temporal clause; گرد here is 'dust' and anticipates the next verb (خروشید) — so: 'Riders came from the Turks; when the dust (rose)...'.
segments.s667-g16: خروشید is a past tense verb 'they roared/cried aloud.' کای is a vocative 'O' (archaic/literary), introducing the words of the cry, and نامداران مرد is the vocative phrase 'renowned men' addressed by the shout. The sequence reports the shout uttered when the dust rose.
segments.s667-g73: This is a short narrative clause in past tense: subject 'فرستاده' (the messenger) + past verb 'آمد' (came) followed by the preposition 'به' + indirect object 'بهرام' (to Bahram) and the verb 'گفت' (said). Word order is the typical subject–verb–(prepositional phrase) with a second verb 'گفت' coordinating the reported speech that follows.
segments.s667-g74: 'که' introduces the content of what was said. 'رازی که داری' is a noun with a relative clause ('the secret that you have') where 'داری' is present-tense 'you have'. 'بر آر' is a verbal complex meaning 'bring forth / reveal' (a classical/poetic verb formed with the prefix بر- + آر) and functions here as an imperative/request. 'از نهفت' literally 'from concealment' (از + نهان/نهفت used adverbially) => 'out of hiding'.
segments.s667-g75: 'که' continues the reported clause. 'این شهریاریست' is a nominal phrase with the copula '-ست' attached to 'شهریاری' (kingship/sovereignty): 'this is kingship.' The predicate 'نیک اختری' is an adjectival phrase 'good / auspicious, star-like' — a poetic epithet placed after the copula.
segments.s667-g76: 'بجوید' is the classical present (3rd-singular) form of 'جستن' (to seek): 'it seeks'. 'همی' is a continuous/aspective particle meaning 'always / continually'. 'چون تو' = 'like you' (چون = like; تو = you) modifies the following noun 'فرمانبری' (obedience/submission), so the phrase means 'it continually seeks obedience like yours.'
segments.s667-g77: 'بدو' = 'to him' (prepositional phrase marking the indirect object). 'گفت' is the simple past 'said'. 'بهرام' is the speaker (Bahram). 'کو را بگوی' is an interrogative fragment: 'کو' (classical/poetic 'who/which/where') + object marker 'را' + 'بگوی' (classical jussive/subjunctive/indefinite form of گفتن here used to express 'shall I tell / should I tell'); together: 'Whom/which shall I tell?'.
segments.s667-g78: 'که‌گر' = 'if' (classical fused conditional 'که گر'). 'رزمجویی' is the agent noun 'one who seeks battle / a seeker of war.' 'بهانه' is the noun 'excuse / pretext.' 'مجوی' is a prohibitive/negative jussive: 'do not seek' (addressing the addressee). So the clause means 'If you are a seeker of battle, do not look for an excuse.'
segments.s667-g79: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'ایدون' is an adverb meaning 'henceforth / from now on' in older/poetic Persian. 'که' links the conditional clause. 'با شهریار جهان' is a prepositional phrase 'with the sovereign (شهریار) of the world (جهان).' Together they set up the condition 'If from now on (it is) that (you are) with the sovereign of the world…' (i.e. 'If henceforth you... with the world's sovereign').
segments.s667-g80: 'همی' again gives continuous/aspective force ('ever/now'). 'آشتی‌جویی' is the noun/verb-form 'seeking reconciliation / making peace' (here functioning as the action you undertake). 'اندر نهان' is a locative/adverbial phrase: 'in secret / within concealment' (اندر = in; نهان = hidden/secretly). The whole segment reads '(you) seek reconciliation in secret.'
segments.s667-g289: همه (all) + شارستانهای (cities, plural) + گیتی (the world) form a noun phrase 'all the cities of the world'. مراست is a fused/poetic form meaning 'مرا است' = 'is mine/belongs to me' (verb phrase placed after the noun phrase). Word order is the normal Persian NP + verbal predicate, but the verb is poetically fused.
segments.s667-g290: زمانه (time, fate/age) is the subject; برین بر is a poetic spelling of بر این (on/with regard to this) where ین here is the orthographic variant of 'این'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that I said'; گفتم is the past 'I said'; گواست is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'گواه است' = 'is witness'. Overall: 'Time is witness to what I said about this.'
segments.s667-g291: سوی (toward) is a preposition governing شارستانها (the towns, plural). گشادست is a poetic contraction of 'گشاد است' (is wide); here the copula/ adjective precedes the noun (inversion for poetic effect) and راه (road) names what is wide: 'the road toward the towns is wide.'
segments.s667-g292: This clause is elliptical/poetic: چه introduces a free/indefinite contrast ('whether' / 'what'), کهتر (the inferior/smaller one) is used substantively, and 'ب دان مرز پوید' is a poetic splitting of 'بدان مرز پوید' = 'hurries to that border' where ب is the short form of به (to), دان here functions as part of 'بدان' (to that). پوید is the present/hepatic verb 'hastens' or 'goes quickly'. The final چه شاه repeats the contrast with 'or the king'.
segments.s667-g293: اگر establishes a conditional ('if'). تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you' (poetic 'thou'). بکوبی is the present/subjunctive form of کوبیدن here meaning 'you batter/knock/force (your way)'; در شارستان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/at the city'. The clause reads 'If you batter at the city...'.
segments.s667-g294: به شاهی نیابی: به شاهی = 'to kingship / royal rule' (the goal); نیابی is negative/resultant present (you will not attain/find). مگر introduces an exception/limitation but here means 'only' or 'except that' and is followed by خارستان 'a thorny thicket'—i.e. 'you will find no kingship, only thorns.'
segments.s667-g295: دگر آنک = 'the other/that which' (آنک is a relative 'that which'); بخشیدنی is a verbal adjective/gerund meaning 'to be pardoned / grantable' (بخشیدن passive/gerundial form); خواستی is past tense 'you wanted' or 'you wished'. Together: 'If you wished that other thing to be pardoned...' (دگر introduces an alternative).
segments.s667-g296: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / by'; مردی in this collocation means 'manliness' or 'manly virtue' (abstract noun). مرا دوری آراستی is a split of 'مرا دوری آراستی' = 'you have made me distant' where 'مرا' (me) is the object (here split into م + را in the provided tokens), دوری = 'distance / estrangement', and آراستی is past 'you adorned/arranged/made', so 'you have made me distant from manliness.'
segments.s667-g65: چو functions as a conditional 'if' here. خشنود is an adjective 'pleased'; باشد is the 3rd-person subjunctive/present form 'be / is (may be)' used in classical Persian. ز من ('from/by me') marks the cause/beneficiary of that pleasure. شایدم is a fused poetic form of شاید + م meaning 'perhaps/it may be' and reinforces the uncertainty of the clause.
segments.s667-g66: اگر introduces a conditional subordinate clause 'if'. خاک بالا is a poetic noun phrase 'high/lofty earth' (i.e. high ground) with خاک as the head and بالا as an adjective. بپیمایدم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form with the prefix بـ (b-) and the final -م ('I may/shall'), here meaning 'I may traverse/scale' or 'I shall climb'. Together the clause expresses a possible action under the condition set before.
segments.s667-g67: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger'; آمد is the verb 'came'. بر + ساوه marks the destination 'to Saveh' (بر here = 'to/toward'). شاه follows as the indirect addressee/target ('to the king' or 'at/for the king') — classical Persian can place the addressee after the place without repeating a preposition.
segments.s667-g68: بگفت is a past verbal form 'he said / he told'. آنچ (= آن + چ) is a relative 'that which' or 'what'. بشنید is past 'he heard' and forms the content clause 'that which he heard'. زان means 'from that / from him' linking the report to its source, and رزمخواه names the source as 'that warrior / aspiring fighter'. The entire segment reports what the messenger related.
segments.s667-g69: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). رو پارسی را بگوی is an imperative-like instruction: رو پارسی = 'in the Persian tongue / in Persian' (رو here marks the manner/way of speech) and را is the definite/object marker; بگوی is an imperative or jussive 'say / speak'. The order reflects classical Persian: imperative verb at the end with its object marked by را.
segments.s667-g70: که introduces the content/explanatory clause 'that / for'. چندین چرا literally 'many whys' = 'many reasons' (an idiomatic plural). بایدت is باید + enclitic ت 'you must/ought' (modal obligation directed to the addressee). گفت و گوی is a paired expression formed from two cognates 'to say' (گفت) and 'to speak/tell' (گوی) used here as a hortatory or emphatic repetition meaning 'state and speak them' (i.e., give and tell the reasons).
segments.s667-g71: چرا آمده‌ستی is a direct question 'why have you come?' with آمده‌ستی the 2nd-person perfect/present-past form 'you have come/you came'. بدین بارگاه corresponds to 'to this court/palace' (بدین = ب + این, 'to this' in classical usage; بارگاه = 'royal court/palace'). The phrase asks the purpose of the visitor's arrival at the court.
segments.s667-g72: ز ما = 'from us' (z = from/of; ما = us). آرزو هرچ = 'whatever desire/wish' (هرچ = 'whatever'). باید is the modal 'you ought / it is necessary' and precedes the verb; بخواه is the imperative/subjunctive 'ask (for), request' (2nd-person). Together the clause instructs the visitor to request any wish they need from the king.
segments.s667-g129: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. من زینهاری بود is a past copular clause: زینهاری (an agentive/nominal form of زنهار, 'warning' or 'one who warns') is predicate and بود is the past copula 'was' — here = 'I warned' or 'I was a warner'. ننگ تو ('your shame') is a nominative/plain noun phrase that connects to the following clause (it is the theme/thing affected by the warning).
segments.s667-g130: بدین خردگی is a prepositional/advantage phrase meaning 'in/with this smallness/humiliation' (بدین = 'with/in this', خردگی = 'smallness, humiliation'). کردم is the 1st-person past 'I made/did' and takes آهنگ تو ('my/your aim/intention toward you') as its object: literally 'I made (کردم) your aim (آهنگ تو) in this humility'. The poetic word-order places the adverbial phrase before the verb.
segments.s667-g131: نبینی is the 2nd-person negative present/predictive form of دیدن ('you do not / will not see'). م + را here represent the object pronoun مرا ('me') split in the tokenizer; را is the definite/object marker attached to the object. جز = 'except' and به‌روز نبرد = 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز 'on the day', نبرد 'battle'). The whole clause means 'You will not see me except on the day of battle.'
segments.s667-g132: درفشی is the banner/standard (subject). پس here functions as 'then/after' or an emphatic sequence marker linking the following locative phrase; پشت من = 'at my back/behind me' (پشت + possessive من). لاژورد is a color/substance 'lapis-lazuli/azure' describing the banner. Word order is noun (banner) + locative predicate ('behind me was lapis-lazuli').
segments.s667-g133: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'for/that'. دیدار آن اژدها = 'the meeting/sight of that dragon' (دیدار = 'meeting/sight of', آن = 'that', اژدها = 'dragon'). مرگ تست is a predicative phrase meaning 'is death for you' — تست here is the archaic 2nd-person copula ('thou art' = 'is yours') in the sense of 'is for you' (note: the token list gave non-matching modern senses; grammatically this is the copular construction indicating possession/experiencer).
segments.s667-g134: نیام سنانم is a genitive construction: نیام 'sheath/scabbard' + سنانم 'of my spear' (سنان + possessive -م). The following سر و ترگ تست is a verbless/predicate phrase with the archaic copula تست: 'is your head and your rag(s)'. In other words the scabbard of my spear is (to be) your head and your rags — a terse threat that the foe's head and garments will be taken into the sheath. The conjunction و links 'head' and 'rag'.
segments.s667-g135: چو = 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. گفتارهای درشت is the object: گفتارهای (plural 'words/speeches/utterances') modified by درشت ('coarse/harsh/rough'). The clause thus means 'When he heard the harsh words...'.
segments.s667-g136: فرستاده ساوه is 'the envoy/messenger of Saveh' (فرستاده = 'messenger', ساوه = the place name Saveh). بنمود پشت is a light-verb construction meaning 'showed his back' or idiomatically 'turned his back' (بنمود = 'showed/made appear', پشت = 'back'). In context it reports that the Saveh envoy turned his back (left/withdrew).
segments.s667-g177: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'once again' or 'on another occasion' and precedes the noun phrase it modifies. گردی here functions as a nominalized action—'a turning/coming' or 'a roaming/round'—that combines with زبان‌آوری (a compound meaning 'eloquence' or 'speech-making'), so گردی زبان‌آوری = 'a round/coming of speech' (i.e. an outbreak/instance of eloquence). Word order (adv + nominal + noun-compound) is poetic but grammatical.
segments.s667-g178: فریبنده is an adjective meaning 'deceptive/enticing' that in this poetic line precedes the noun مردی ('a man'), yielding 'a deceptive man.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and links that man to the place-noun compound دشت هری ('the plain of Hari/Herat'). The whole chunk is an adjectival phrase identifying the source of the eloquence.
segments.s667-g179: فرستاد is a past-tense verb 'he sent/dispatched' (subject is implicit from context). نزدیک بهرام is an adverbial phrase 'to/near Bahram' indicating direction or proximity of the sending. و links the actions and گفت is the second past verb 'and said,' introducing reported speech that follows (the clause begun by که).
segments.s667-g180: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the reported content. بخت سپهری is a noun phrase 'celestial/astral fortune' where سپهری qualifies بخت. تو را uses تو with the object/topic marker را (here marking the person affected), and نیست جفت is the predicate 'is not (there) a pair/match'—literally 'for you there is no pair.' The copula نیست negates existence of a mate; the construction is idiomatic/archaic Persian for 'there is no match for you.'
segments.s667-g181: همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle (here functioning like 'do/indeed/still'), and بشنوی is a hortative/present-subjunctive or imperative-like form 'hear/listen.' چند quantifies 'a few/some' and governs the coordinated direct object پند و سخن ('advice and words'). Note the verb (بشنوی) comes before its objects, a common classical order.
segments.s667-g182: This line contains two imperatives: خرد یار کن is literally 'make wisdom a friend/companion' (یار کن = 'make (someone) a friend/companion'), where خرد is the thing to be allied with you. چشم دل بازکن is another imperative pair: چشم دل 'the eye of the heart' (a genitive compound) plus بازکن 'open!' — 'open the heart's eye.' The verbs are direct imperatives addressed to the addressee.
segments.s667-g183: دو تن is the direct object 'two persons' (تن here = 'person') placed before the verb for emphasis. یافته‌ستی is the second-person perfect 'you have found' (یافته + ستی). که begins the relative/subordinate clause 'such that' and اندر جهان sets the location 'in the world' for what follows.
segments.s667-g184: چو ایشان نبود is an archaic negative construction meaning 'there was not anyone like them' (چو = 'as/like', ایشان = 'they', نبود = 'there was not'). از نژاد introduces origin 'from the lineage/stock of ...' (نژاد = 'lineage'), and مهان here names that class (an older poetic term; in context it functions as a group/quality). The clause thus says: 'in the world there were none of that lineage equal to them.'
segments.s667-g321: به فرزند گفت is 'he said to (his) son' with به marking the indirect object (to/for) followed by the noun فرزند; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing the speech. ای + گزین سپاه is a vocative: ای 'O!' addresses the son, گزین functions as an epithet 'chosen/selected' and سپاه ('the host/army') qualifies that epithet (literally 'chosen of the host'). The vocative follows the reporting verb, as is common in classical style.
segments.s667-g322: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (classical/poetic negative). جنگ here is a verbal-noun sense 'battle/engage in war' (object of the prohibition). تا introduces the temporal limit 'until', and بامداد پگاه are two near-synonyms for early morning/dawn used together for emphasis ('the break of day'). The sequence literally reads 'Do not (meken) [make] war until morning-dawn.'
segments.s667-g323: شدند (third-person plural past) plus the following prepositional phrase expresses 'they became / they were' in the sense 'they returned' — here idiomatically 'they went back to their places.' از introduces origin 'from', دو رویه is 'the two sides/fronts' (رویه = side/front in context), سپه = 'the host/army'. باز is an adverb 'again' and جای 'place' completes the idea 'returned to their places.' Word order places the verb then the source phrase, a poetic/narrative ordering.
segments.s667-g324: طلایه is the subject 'the vanguard/forefront'. بیامد is the past 'came' (came forth). ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from', پرده 'curtain' and سرای here means 'pavilion/house/tent' (the lexical senses given for سرای in the list are different; in context it is a place noun). The clause thus reads 'the vanguard came out from the pavilion's curtain.'
segments.s667-g325: بر افراختند آتش is a verbal phrase 'they raised/erected/kindled fire(s)', with بر as a directional/intensive particle and افراختند the past plural 'they raised'. از هر دو روی is a locative/source phrase 'from each of the two sides' (i.e. from both fronts). The clause means 'they kindled fires on both sides.'
segments.s667-g326: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد is the past copula/become). ز introduces the agent/source 'by/from' (poetic z = از) and لشکر is 'the army/host'. پر (here an adjective) means 'full (of)'; it is followed by از + گفت و گوی, a compound meaning 'of cries/conversation/clamour'. Grammatically the clause says 'the world became, because of the army, full of talk and shouting.'
segments.s667-g327: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the subject; در خیمه 'in the tent' is a locative phrase. تنها 'alone' qualifies the subject. بماند is the past narrative form 'remained' (3rd person singular). The whole clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When Bahram remained alone in the tent...'.
segments.s667-g328: فرستاد is the past 'he sent' (object unexpressed, implied 'messengers' or 'word'). و 'and' links the two actions. ایرانیان را has را as the definite object marker marking 'the Iranians' as direct object, and بخواند here means 'he summoned/called (together)' — a past narrative form used to report that action. Together: 'he sent (word/messengers) and summoned the Iranians.'
segments.s667-g241: This is two coordinated simple clauses: 'فرستاده گفت' (subject + past verb 'said/spoke') joined by the coordinating particle 'و' to the clause 'سپهبد شنید' (title as subject + past verb 'heard'). Both verbs are simple past (perfective). The verb 'گفت' here functions as a lexical past-tense verb (not the nominal sense listed in the lemma data).
segments.s667-g242: 'به پاسخ' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/as a reply', followed by 'سخن تیره' where 'سخن' (speech/word) takes the postpositive adjective 'تیره' (here 'ominous')—a common Persian order is noun + adjective. 'آمد پدید' is a verbal expression meaning 'came forth/appeared' (past tense 'آمد' + predicative/verb 'پدید' = 'appeared').
segments.s667-g243: 'چنین داد پاسخ' is a fixed expression: 'gave such an answer' (داد = past 'gave'). The particle 'که' here introduces direct speech/quotation and is immediately followed by the vocative particle 'ای' addressing the noun 'بدنشان' (their body/bodies). The copula is omitted in the quoted clause (typical in Persian).
segments.s667-g244: 'میان' is the preposition 'between' governing the two coordinated nouns 'بزرگان و گردنکشان' ('the great/elders' and 'the rebels/proud ones'). This phrase is the complement of the vocative phrase started in the previous segment ('بدنشان ... میان ...'), so the structure is effectively 'their bodies — between the great and the rebels.'
segments.s667-g245: A noun 'جهاندار' (sovereign) followed by two adjectival modifiers 'بی‌سود' (without profit/useless) and 'بسیارگوی' (much-speaking, i.e. talkative/loquacious). Adjectives follow the noun in Persian; the coordinating 'و' links the two adjectives.
segments.s667-g246: 'نماندش' is past negative 'نماند' plus enclitic pronoun 'ش' = 'no longer remained for him' (or 'was not left for him'). 'نزد کسی' is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of anyone' and 'آبروی' is the noun 'reputation/honor' that is the thing which 'did not remain'. Word order places the verb with its clitic before the prepositional phrase and the patient noun.
segments.s667-g247: The preposition 'به' marks the topic 'regarding' (here: 'with respect to the earlier speech'), 'پیشین سخن' = 'the prior/earlier speech', and 'و آنچ گفتی ز پس' = 'and that which you said afterwards' (ز پس = 'after that'/'since then'). 'گفتی' is the 2nd-person past tense 'you said' even though the lemma senses given are nominal; it's functioning as a verb here.
segments.s667-g248: 'به گفتار' is an adverbial phrase 'in/with respect to speech' (literal 'in speech'). 'دیدم' is first-person past 'I saw/found', with 'تو را' as the object ('I found you'), and 'دسترس' as a predicative complement meaning 'accessible/within reach' (i.e. available or apt in speech). Persian commonly places the verb before the object + complement as here.
segments.s667-g409: بیامد is the simple past, 3rd-person singular (“he came”). به introduces the indirect object/place (“to”). خراد برزین is a personal name (here written as two words: خراد as an element meaning “wise” or part of the name, برزین the proper name). بگفت is an archaic/poetic past form of گفت (he said); the prefix ب- here is part of the classical narrative style and functions like a simple past verb (“and said”). Word order is older/epic Persian: verb + prepositional phrase + name + verb.
segments.s667-g410: که introduces the reported clause (“that”). بهرام را puts Bahram in focus/topic with the object/definite marker را. نیست is the negative existential/predicative (‘is not’ / ‘there is no…’). جز means “except” and introduces the exception; دیو جفت is a compound noun (دیو ‘demon’ + جفت ‘match/pair’) meaning roughly “a matched demon” or “a demon-pair.” The whole pattern نیست جز X is a classical formula meaning “is nothing but X.”
segments.s667-g411: دبیران is the plural subject “the secretaries.” بجستند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural (‘they sought’). راه گریز is a noun phrase where راه (‘way/path’) is modified by گریز (‘escape’), producing “a way of escape” or “escape-route.” The syntax is Subject–Verb–Object in this older style.
segments.s667-g412: بدان is a fused form (ب- + دان) functioning adverbially = “to that / for that purpose,” referring back to the escape-route; this is an older poetic construction. تا introduces purpose/result (“so that / in order that”); نبیند is a negative subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form (‘may/might not see’ or ‘would not see’). کسی with negation yields the sense ‘no one/anyone’ and رستخیز is the noun ‘rally/uprising’ (here meaning an assembling/raising). Together: “for that, so that no one would see the uprising.”
segments.s667-g413: ز is the poetic variant of از and here means “because of / out of” (introducing cause). بیم is the noun ‘fear’ and governs the following nouns (fear of X). شهنشاه is ‘the king/emperor.’ و links the two causes (the king and Bahram). بهرام شیر is a noun phrase in apposition: بهرام followed by شیر ‘the Lion’ (an epithet). The whole phrase means “because of fear of the king and of Bahram the Lion.”
segments.s667-g414: تلی is the object ‘a heap / mound.’ برگزیدند is the past 3rd-person plural ‘they chose.’ هر دو دبیر follows as the subject in emphatic/poetic word order and means ‘both secretaries’ or ‘each of the two secretaries’ (here best read ‘both secretaries’). So literally: ‘They chose a mound — both secretaries.’
segments.s667-g415: یکی is an indefinite/ordinal “one” (a certain one / one of them). تند بالا forms an adverbial phrase ‘swiftly upward / quickly up’ with تند modifying بالا. بد here is an archaic/poetic element whose exact lexical sense is not represented in the provided senses (it functions as a verbal/auxiliary element meaning roughly ‘went’ or ‘moved’ in context); از رزم دور is a prepositional phrase ‘from the distant battle’ (از = from, رزم = battle, دور = far/distant). The segment describes a quick motion away from the battlefield.
segments.s667-g416: به یک‌سو is a directional phrase ‘toward one side / aside.’ ز راه is ‘from the way/road’ (ز = from, راه = road). سواران تور is a noun phrase ‘the horsemen of Tur’ (سواران = riders/horsemen, تور = the proper name Tūr/Tur), so the whole phrase means ‘toward one side, off the road of Tur’s horsemen.’ Word order places the direction first, then the source/possessive group.
segments.s667-g401: بدین is a poetic prepositional compound (literally 'by/with this' = به + این) introducing a cause; جنگ تنگی is a noun phrase where تنگی ('hardship, distress') is the main noun and جنگ ('war') qualifies it (the hardship caused by war). به ایران is the indirect-object/dative phrase 'to Iran', and شود is the simple present/subjunctive/future-like verb 'becomes/happens' placed at the end as Persian verbs normally are.
segments.s667-g402: بوم ما is the possessed noun 'our homeland' (noun + possessive pronoun: بوم + ما). پاک is an adverbial/intensifying word here meaning 'entirely/completely', modifying the following adjective. ویران شود is a nominal-predicate + verb 'be ruined' (ویران = ruined/desolate; شود = 'becomes/is made'), with the verb at the clause end as usual in Persian. The initial برو appears as part of the compound phrase with بوم (poetic/archaic) and functions as part of the noun phrase 'برو بوم' = 'homeland' (see note below).
segments.s667-g403: نه ... نه ... نه is negative coordination 'neither ... nor ... nor'. خاکست پیدا is a verbless/predicative phrase where پید ا functions as the predicate 'is seen/visible, is found' and خاکست (ashes) is the item said not to appear. The following نه دریا نه کوه continue the coordinated list of things not to be seen; nouns precede the predicate in this poetic order.
segments.s667-g404: ز بس is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from/through so great a number' (ز = از 'from', بس = 'much/plenty'). تیغ داران is a compound meaning 'sword-holders' (تیغ + داران, a plural agentive), then توران specifies their origin ('of Turan'), and گروه ('troop/band') is the head noun indicating these are a troop. The whole phrase gives the cause/agent for the previous negation.
segments.s667-g405: یکی is an indefinite subject particle 'one/someone' introducing an actor; برخروشید is a past tense verb meaning 'rose up in fury / shouted' (bar‑khorushid, poetic past). بهرام follows as a proper-name apposition identifying that someone. سخت is an adverb/adjunct 'fiercely/strongly' modifying how Bahram rose, and its placement after the name is a typical poetic order.
segments.s667-g406: ورا is the old/poetic form meaning 'to him/unto him' (indirect object). گفت here is the verb 'said' (the form is past), and کای is the vocative particle 'O'. بد دل شوربخت is a vocative noun phrase (بد دل 'evil/ill‑hearted', شوربخت 'unfortunate, wretched') addressed to the addressee. The vocative + epithet follows the reporting verb, a common pattern.
segments.s667-g407: تو را uses topical/emphatic marking (تو 'you' + را marking the topic/object). از + predicate (دواتست) is a proverbial/idiomatic nominal clause 'you are of the ink‑stand' (دواتست = 'are inkstand' = 'belong to the pen/ink' = 'a scribe' in context). و قرطاس بر coordinates another nominal 'and paper upon' (قرطاس = paper); the verbless copular sense is encoded in the compounded forms (دواتست). The order is poetic: topic + predicate(s) before an explicit copula.
segments.s667-g408: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the army' (ز = poetic from/of). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...', and گفتت is the past verb 'said to you' (گفت + enclitic ت = 'he said to you'). The following که introduces reported content 'that مردم شمر' (literally 'that the people [are] Shamar' — a verbless reported clause where شمر is a predicate/noun identifying the people). The sequence is a nested reported clause introduced by که.
segments.s667-g81: تو را: 'تو' is the second-person singular pronoun and 'را' is the definite/object marker attached after it, so this marks 'you' as the direct object. اندرین مرز: 'اندرین' is an archaic/poetic locative meaning 'in this' or 'in these', followed by 'مرز' (border/frontier). مهمان کنم: verb phrase with the root 'کردن' in the 1st person (کنم) plus 'مهمان' as the object/predicative — literally 'I make (you) a guest' = 'I will host you'; the form کنم is the classical present/subjunctive used for a future or volitional sense.
segments.s667-g82: به چیزی: prepositional phrase 'به' + 'چیزی' = 'for something' / 'for whatever'; که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'چیزی'. گویی تو فرمان کنم: 'گویی' here functions as a subordinate verb meaning 'that you say' / 'that you ask' (an archaic/political use corresponding to modern 'بگویی'/'بگویی که'); 'فرمان کنم' is a volitional/subjunctive clause 'I shall command/grant' with کنم marking the speaker's willingness — the whole clause = 'which you say I should command/grant'.
segments.s667-g83: ببخشم: first-person verb form from بخشیدن; here it means 'I will give/bestow' (the form is subjunctive/volitional used for future-giving). سپاه تو را: 'سپاه' (army) with the possessive pronoun 'تو' follows, and 'را' marks the phrase as the object receiving the gift; word order (verb before object) is common in classical Persian poetry. سیم و زر: the direct objects given — 'silver and gold'.
segments.s667-g84: کرا: an archaic relative/pronominal particle ('که را' / 'which/that') referring back to the 'سیم و زر' just mentioned. درخور آید: fixed expression 'درخور آمدن/آییدن' = 'to befit' or 'to be appropriate' — آید is 3rd-person present/subjunctive/present used idiomatically. کلاه و کمر: the items 'helmet (کلاه) and belt/girdle (کمر)', i.e. the military accoutrements that the silver and gold are suitable for.
segments.s667-g85: سواری فرستیم: 'سواری' (a rider/horseman) is the direct object; 'فرستیم' is 1st-person plural (classical hortative/volitional) 'we/I will send' — in poetic narration this expresses the speaker's action of dispatching a mounted messenger. نزدیک شاه: adverbial phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک after the verb marking destination/position).
segments.s667-g86: بـدان تا: here 'بـدان' is an archaic locative/adverbial compound (literally 'بِ + آن' → 'to that place/there' or 'to him/there'); 'تا' introduces purpose/result 'so that'. به راه: 'on/along the road'; آیدت: 'آید' (3rd-person present/subjunctive) + enclitic 'ت' = 'may come to you' or 'come toward you' ('آمدن' used in the sense 'to meet'); نیم راه: 'halfway' (نیم + راه) — the whole clause means 'so that (someone) may come out halfway along the road to meet you.'
segments.s667-g87: بسان همالان: 'بسان' = 'like/as'; 'همالان' is the plural of 'همال' meaning 'porters/carriers' — so 'like the porters'. علف سازدت: 'علف' = 'fodder/grass'; 'سازدت' is an archaic causative/past-future-form of 'ساختن' with enclitic 'ت' (you) meaning 'he/they will make you' or 'he will reduce you to' — together 'make you fodder' (i.e. treat you as fodder/lowly).
segments.s667-g88: اگر دوستی شاه بنوازدت: 'اگر' introduces the condition 'if'; 'دوستی' here means 'friendship' or 'favour' and 'شاه' is its possessor ('the king's favour'). 'بنوازدت' is composed of the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form of 'نوازیدن' (to caress, favor, treat kindly) with the enclitic 'ت' = 'you' — 'if the king's favour should caress/treat you kindly' (in context the clause sets the condition affecting how you will be treated by others).
segments.s667-g257: نگوید is an injunction here (‘do not say’), followed by the subordinating که introducing the clause. جز marks exception (‘except’); مهتر ده is a noun phrase ‘the village head’ (مهتر + ده ‘village’). بدم is an archaic/poetic first‑person past copula (compare بودم) meaning ‘I was’ (so the whole clause = “Do not say that, except that I was the village‑head”).
segments.s667-g258: همه بنده بودند is a simple past statement ‘everyone was/they were servants’ (همه = ‘all’; بنده بودند = ‘were servants’). و = ‘and’. من is the first‑person pronoun ‘I’. مه بدم is a poetic verblike phrase: مه here is emphatic/major (‘great’/chief) andبدم functions as the archaic 1st‑person past copula (cf. بودم) so the sense is ‘and I was (the) chief/important one’.
segments.s667-g259: بدین (به این) = ‘by/with/in this’ (introducing the matter). کار ما = ‘our task/this business’. برنیاید is an impersonal/negative verbal phrase meaning ‘it will not come about / it is not fitting/possible’ for us; دو روز is the duration ‘in two days’ placed after the verb. Together: ‘This task cannot be done by us in two days.’
segments.s667-g260: که introduces the subordinate clause of result/purpose (‘such that/so that’). بفروزد is 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative of فروزیدن ‘to shine/kindle’ (here ‘may shine’). از چرخ گیتی = ‘from the wheel/sky of the world’ (چرخ = sky/heaven, گیتی = world). فروز is an adjective/epithet ‘bright/illuminating’ describing the resulting shining. Word order is poetic: verb then source phrase then descriptive epithet.
segments.s667-g261: که again introduces a relative/result clause. بر نیزه‌ها = ‘on/onto the spears’ (بر + plural نیزه‌ها). بر سرت = ‘over/above your head’ (second prepositional phrase stacked for emphasis). خون فشان is a participial/epithet ‘blood‑spraying’ or ‘blood‑shedding’ that modifies the implied agents/objects: poetically it describes the bloody spectacle to be set up on spears over the head.
segments.s667-g262: فرستم is 1st‑person present/volitive of فرستادن used to express ‘I will send’ or ‘I shall send’. بر marks the target ‘upon/against’. شاه گردنکشان is the target phrase: شاه ‘king’ with گردنکشان (‘those who pull up/neck‑pullers’ = proud rebels); together ‘upon the rebellious proud ones/kings’ (i.e. I will send them upon the rebellious kings).
segments.s667-g263: دگر = ‘the other/another’; آنک is a relative pronoun ‘that/which’ (introducing the item previously mentioned). گفتی is the past 2nd‑person verb ‘you said/you mentioned’ (here used with تو as subject). از دخترت = ‘about/ concerning your daughter’ (preposition از + possessed noun). The clause means ‘the other thing that you said about your daughter’.
segments.s667-g264: هم = ‘also/too’. از گنج = ‘concerning/from the treasure’ (از introducing the topic). وز here is the fused poetic form of ‘و ز’ (and of/from), used to link items in a list; it functions as ‘and (of)’. لشکر = ‘army/host’. و = ‘and’. کشورت = ‘your country/realm’ (possessed form). Together: ‘also about the treasure, and about the army and your realm.’
segments.s667-g217: تو را is the topic/object marked phrase ('you' + را) placing 'you' as the one concerned; بر تن خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'on/for your own body' (بر = on/upon + تن خویشتن = your own body); مهر نیست is an existential negation 'there is no love/affection'. Together the clause means 'you have no affection for your own body.'
segments.s667-g218: وگر introduces a conditional 'and if'; هست مهر تو را is an inversion meaning 'there is love for you' (هست = there is/exist, مهر تو را = love for you); چهر نیست places the predicate after the topic: 'there is no face/appearance.' The clause says 'and if there is love for you, that love has no face/appearance.'
segments.s667-g219: که here gives the explanatory 'because/that'; نشناسدی is 2nd-person singular past negative 'you did not recognize' (ن- prefix + شناسدی); چشم او is a possessive construction 'his eye' (noun + pronoun); نیک و بد is a coordinated complement 'good and bad', implying 'whether good or bad.'
segments.s667-g220: گزاف is an adjective/adverbial 'groundless/rash'; از خرد یافته is a small noun phrase 'obtained/derived from reason' (از = from, خرد = reason/wisdom, یافته = found/derived); کی سزد is a rhetorical question 'when is it fitting?' (کی = when/ever, سزد = 'it befits/it is fitting'). The whole says that a rash judgment derived from reason is not fitting.
segments.s667-g221: بپرهیز is an imperative 'refrain/avoid' (2nd sing. command); زین is poetic/contracted 'from this' (ز + این) and governs جنگ 'this battle'; و links the clauses; پیش من آی is 'come before me/come into my presence' with آی as an exhortation 'come!' The clause orders the addressee to desist and to approach the speaker.
segments.s667-g222: نمانم is first-person present/subjunctive with negative prefix 'ن-' meaning 'I will not remain' or 'I will not be left'; که introduces the purpose/reason 'so that/that'; مانی here is the 2nd-person form of ماندن 'you remain' (archaic/poetic spelling), meaning 'you should/you might remain' — note the supplied lemma list treats مانی as a name, but in context it functions as the verb 'you remain'; زمانی به پای = 'for a time at (my) feet' (زمانی = for a while, به = at/to, پای = foot/feet).
segments.s667-g223: تو را is the indirect object 'to you' (topic/recipient marked by را); کدخدایی و دختر دهم is a coordinated object phrase '(the) headmanship and a daughter I will give' — the verb دهم ('I give') applies to both nouns. The clause promises offices and a daughter to the addressee.
segments.s667-g224: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'the same' or 'that same' modifying ارجمندی 'dignity/honor'; و links the next gift; اختر دهم repeats the verb 'I give (a) star' — the verb دهم is shared with the preceding clause, so the speaker promises honor and a star as well.
segments.s667-g105: بگویش is the imperative بگو + enclitic ـش meaning “to him” (tell him). که introduces a content clause (“that …”). در جنگ تو نیست نام is a negated existential statement with predicate نام (“fame/reputation”) placed after the verb; literally “in battle (در جنگ) for you (تو) there is not (نیست) a name/fame.” Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: locative + possessor + copula + predicate.
segments.s667-g106: نه is the coordinating negative “nor” linking to the previous clause. از کشتنت is a prepositional phrase “from/by your killing” where کشتنت is the deverbal + clitic indicating the object “you” (i.e. ‘killing you’). نیز here reinforces the negation (“also/indeed not”). یابیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative form (we may/shall find); followed by کام (“satisfaction/wish”), the object: “nor shall we find satisfaction from your killing.”
segments.s667-g107: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction “when/if.” شاه تو is a noun + possessor “your king.” بر در مرا = “at my gate/door”; in classical Persian مرا here forms the possessive phrase ‘my …’ (the tokenization splits م and را). کهترند is a 3rd‑person plural present predicate “are inferior/lesser.” The clause therefore says that, in the situation described, those present are inferior at the speaker’s gate.
segments.s667-g108: تو را is a dative/contrast marker meaning “to you / as for you.” کمترین is the superlative “the least” and modifies چاکران “servants/attendants” (adjective before noun). مهترند is the verb phrase “they are greater/superior” (3rd‑person plural). The clause states a comparison: even the humblest attendants are superior in relation to you.
segments.s667-g109: گر is the conditional particle “if.” ایدون (classical form) = “come (now/hither)” — an archaic/past-present verb form meaning ‘come.’ که introduces the subordinate clause expressing the content of the condition. زنهار خواهی ز من is “you desire/ask for zenehār from me”; here زنهار functions as an entreaty/petition (‘pardon/mercy’ or ‘forbearance’) and خواهی is 2nd‑person present/future “you want/ask,” with ز + من = “from me.” The whole segment = “If you come now asking mercy from me ….”
segments.s667-g110: سرت is the object “your head” (سر + enclitic ـت). برگذارم is 1st‑person singular (I will …) of an older/poetic verb form; here it expresses a forcible act toward the head (contextually “remove/separate/take off”), so the literal sequence is “I will (do to) your head ….” ازین انجمن = “from this assembly” (از = from, ین = orthographic variant of این “this,” انجمن = assembly/gathering). The clause promises a violent separation of the head from the company: “I will remove your head from this assembly.”
segments.s667-g111: فراوان is an adverb 'in great amount/abundantly' modifying بیابی. بیابی is second-person present/subjunctive or simple present used here with future/optative force 'you will find' / 'you find'. زِ / ز + من is the poetic preposition 'from/of me' (ز = از). خواسته is the past-participial/adjectival form 'desired/asked' (i.e., 'things asked of me'). The phrase therefore means 'you will find many things asked of me' or 'you will find many requests from me.'
segments.s667-g112: شود is the impersonal/predictive form of شدن 'become' often used as 'will be' / 'shall be'. لشکرت is 'your army' (لشکر + second-person suffix -ت). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/utterly' modifying آراسته, which is a past-participle/adjectival 'adorned/prepared', so the predicate means 'your army will become entirely prepared/equipped.' Word order places the auxiliary/finite verb first in this short clause (classical poetic ordering).
segments.s667-g345: همانگاه is an adverbial temporal particle ('immediately') placed at the start. خراد برزین is a personal designation: خراد functions as an epithet/qualifier ('wise') attached to the name برزین (poetic name order is allowed). ز راه is a prepositional phrase ('from/along the road') with the archaic short preposition ز = 'from'.
segments.s667-g346: بیامد is the main past verb ('came'). که introduces a subordinate/relative clause explaining the reason or the person ('who/for he'), here linking to the preceding name. بگریخت is a past-form verb meaning 'fled'; از + ساوه marks the source/place ('from Saveh'); شاه is the title 'the king' and here combines with ساوه to mean 'the king at/of Saveh'.
segments.s667-g347: همی گفت is a durative/continuous past portrayal: همی + گفت = 'he kept saying' or 'he was saying'. ازان چاره is the object/topic ('about that remedy/solution'); اندر گریز is a locative/nominal phrase ('in/through flight' = 'by fleeing' or 'that the remedy is flight'), so the whole segment reports the repeated assertion that the solution is flight.
segments.s667-g348: ازان modifies both following nouns: 'from that...' — لشکر گشن is a noun + adjective ('a hungry/forlorn army' where the adjective follows the noun in a poetic order), و links to آن رستخیز ('and that uprising/resurgence'). The whole phrase specifies what they should flee from.
segments.s667-g349: که begins this explanatory clause. کس درجهان (= 'anyone in the world') is the universal subject ('no one in the world' in the negative construction that follows). زان فزونتر سپاه is a comparative phrase: زان = 'than that/of those', فزونتر = 'more numerous', سپاه = 'army/host' — together 'an army more numerous than those'.
segments.s667-g350: نبیند here functions as the negated/declarative verb in the clause ('(one) does not see' or 'there is no one who sees'); که introduces a relative clause modifying سپاه ('that are with...'). هستند با ساوه شاه = 'that are with the Shah of Saveh' (هستند is the plural copula 'are', با marks accompaniment, ساوه شاه = 'the king at/of Saveh').
segments.s667-g351: به بهرام گفت = 'he said to Bahram' (به = 'to', followed by the addressee's name). ازچه is an interrogative 'why'; سخت ایمنی is a noun phrase meaning 'strict/harsh security' or 'such strict precautions' (سخت intensifies ایمنی). The segment therefore reports his question about the severity of their precautions.
segments.s667-g352: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (watch/guard) + کن (do) = 'keep watch' or 'guard!' بدین دام آهرمنی is the prepositional/oblative phrase specifying the object: بدین (classical compound = 'with/against this' = به + این) + دام 'snare/trap', and آهرمنی is an adjective ('Ahrimanic/demonic') modifying دام. The whole gives the command to watch against this demonic trap.
segments.s667-g273: کنون (adverb) is placed clause-initial to mean 'now.' نیِزهِ من is noun + possessive (noun before the enclitic pronoun من, a common poetic order) = 'my spear.' به + گوشت uses the preposition به with a body-part noun (گوشت, 'flesh') to mark the endpoint/target of رسیدن; رسید is a simple past intransitive 'reached.'
segments.s667-g274: سرت را: سرت (your head) + را (definite/direct-object marker) marks the thing affected. به خنجر uses به instrumentally ('with a dagger'). بخواهم + برید is an older/poetic future/intentional construction where بخواهم ('I will/wish') precedes the verb برید to express intent/future ('I will cut'); the main lexical verb is بریدن 'to cut' (here expressed with the past-stem).
segments.s667-g275: چو رفتی is a temporal subordinating clause: چو + past verb رفتی = 'when you have gone' / 'when you are gone.' The following coordinated list سر و تاج و گنجت ('head and crown and your treasure') is the logical subject/theme; مراست functions as the predicate (classical contraction of مَرا است) meaning 'are mine.' Note the possessive clitic -ت on گنج marks 'your treasure.'
segments.s667-g276: همان is a demonstrative 'that very' or 'the same.' دختر و برده is a coordinated noun phrase 'maiden and slave.' رنجت ('rânjat' = رنج + ـت) here means 'your toil/hardship' and is used as a modifier/appositive to indicate these persons are the result/fruit of the addressee's effort; مراست again (= مَرا است) is the predicate 'are mine.' Classical poetry often compacts these relations (modifier after noun without overt ezâfe).
segments.s667-g277: دگر آنک گفتی is a classical relative/nominalizing turn: دگر 'the other/another' + آنک (relative 'that which/which') + گفتی ('you said') = 'the other thing you said.' فزون از شمار is an adjectival phrase 'greater/more than number' = 'more than can be counted' and comments on the preceding nominalized remark.
segments.s667-g278: مَرا تاج و تخت است (here written compactly) expresses possession: مَرا (for me / mine) + [تاج و تخت] + است ('is/are'). Classical Persian often contracts است onto the following noun (تختست) or writes the possessive pronoun fronted (مرا). The coordinated list پیل و سوار ('elephants and horsemen') continues the predicate: 'the crown and throne and elephants and riders are mine.'
segments.s667-g279: برین داستان = بر + این + داستان 'on/at this tale/point' (literary prepositional phrase). زد here is the classical verb used with داستان (داستان زدن) meaning 'utter/recite/strike into the tale' or more loosely 'came forward / took the stage' and is followed by یکی نامدار 'a certain renowned one' (یکی = 'a certain one/someone', نامدار postposed adjective 'renowned'). Word order is flexible and poetic: prepositional phrase + object + verb + subject-like phrase.
segments.s667-g280: که is a relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing the relative clause. پیچان شد is a verbal phrase: پیچان (twisting/whirling) + شد (became/did) — here 'he turned/whirled.' اندر صف کارزار = 'into the battle-line' (اندر = in/into, صف = battle-line/formation, کارزار = battle). Together the clause says the renowned man suddenly wheeled into the ranks of battle.
segments.s667-g297: چو here begins a conditional clause meaning 'if/when'. بینی is the 2nd-person present form 'you see' (subject 'you' implied). سنانم is سنان + ـم (my spear) with the possessive suffix; word order is verb after the conditional particle. ببخشاییم is an archaic optative/1pl present form derived from بخشیدن (here an entreaty/offer: 'pardon/grant mercy'); the 1pl ending reflects a polite or formulaic plural (’we’ as speaker or an imperial plural) rather than literal plurality.
segments.s667-g298: همان picks up and points back to what was just said ('that same' / 'then'). زیردستی is a noun meaning 'an underling, subordination' and here names the role to be avoided. نفرماییم is a 1st‑person plural negative/future‑or‑subjunctive‑flavored form of فرمودن/فرمـا denoting 'we will not make/appoint/command' (a formulaic, somewhat archaic verbal form); syntactically it gives the main clause predicate for the previous objects.
segments.s667-g299: This is a nominal construction listing two objects with the object marker را after the possessor: سپاه تو را کام = 'the army of yours, (its) desire' and راه تو را = 'your road/way'. The particle را here marks these noun phrases as definite/emphatic objects in a verbless or elliptical clause where the predicate is supplied by the next segment.
segments.s667-g300: همان resumes as a demonstrative/predicative 'the same' (i.e. 'let them be the same/as stated'). زنده modifies پیلان (the old plural of پیل) meaning 'living elephants'. و گاه تو را appends another definite/emphatic NP ('and your time/place') with گاه here meaning 'time' or 'occasion' and تو را marked by را; the clause is elliptical: the copula ('be') is omitted as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s667-g301: چو here = 'when'. صف 'battle‑line/formation' is the direct object of the verbal phrase برکشیدم (1st‑person past/perfect 'I drew up/raised'); برکشیدم is a past or perfective verb used idiomatically for 'draw up (ranks)'. ندارم literally 'I do not have' functions idiomatically as 'I have no (concern)' and is followed by the prepositional phrase به چیز 'about anything' (به + چیز = 'about/for a thing').
segments.s667-g302: نیندیشم is the negative present form meaning 'I do not think / I do not consider' (the negative prefix ن- attached to the stem). از لشکرت is a genitive/concern phrase 'of/about your army' (از = 'about/concerning' here). یک پشیز is an idiom meaning 'a trifle / a whit' and is used with the negative to mean 'not the least bit'.
segments.s667-g303: اگر is the conditional 'if'. شهریاری تو is a nominal phrase in which شهریاری ('kingship' / 'sovereignty') is combined with the 2nd‑person suffix/marker تو; here it functions adverbially or vocatively to mean 'as king/sovereign you'. چندین دروغ = 'many a lie / several lies' where چندین is a multiplicative quantifier modifying دروغ.
segments.s667-g304: بگویی is the 2nd‑person subjunctive/present of گفتن 'to say' and here completes the protasis ('if you say/tell ...'). نگیری is the 2nd‑person negative/subjunctive or future‑oriented form of گرفتن meaning 'you will not take/seize' (negative here indicated by ن‑ prefix attached to the verb stem). به گیتی فروغ = 'the world's radiance/glory' where به marks the target and فروغ is the noun 'radiance' or 'splendour' that the subject might hope to seize; together they form the apodosis 'you will not seize the world's glory'.
segments.s667-g417: برفتند is a classical past 3rd‑person plural (‘they went/rode off’). هر دو (‘both’) follows the verb as its logical subject. بران برز راه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase: بران = 'upon that', برز = an adjective 'lofty/high', راه = 'road/way' — together 'upon that lofty road' (poetic word order: demonstrative + adjective + noun).
segments.s667-g418: که ... introduces a subordinate/purposive clause ('in order that / so that'). شاییست is an archaic predicative adjective 'is worthy / befitting' (here with a royal nuance). کردن functions as a light/auxiliary infinitival element ('to do' / part of the infinitive construction). به لشکر نگاه is the infinitival object phrase 'a look/gaze toward the army' (به = toward/to, لشکر = host/army, نگاه = gaze/glance).
segments.s667-g419: نهادند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they placed/they set'. The preposition بر introduces the target ('upon/on'). ترگ is a rare/poetic noun here the object of the preposition (literal gloss 'rag/shred' in the lexicon given, but used as a visible rallying sign/standard in epic contexts). The phrase بهرام چشم is the possessed construction 'Bahram's gaze/eye' (classical Persian often places the possessor after the possessed), so the whole phrase means 'they set Bahram's gaze upon the terg.'
segments.s667-g420: که تا introduces purpose/result ('that / so that'). تا here functions as 'in order that / so that'. چون کند is an embedded interrogative/subordinate clause meaning 'how he will do' (چون = 'how', کند = classical present/subjunctive used for future/habitual action 'does/will do'). جنگ هنگام خشم = 'the fighting/war at the time of anger' (هنگام = 'at the time of', خشم = 'anger'); together 'how he will fight in the hour of wrath.'
segments.s667-g421: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the subject. جنگی modifies سپه (جنگی سپه = 'warlike army/host'). راست کرد is a two‑word verbal phrase 'made straight / set (into order)' — راست = 'straight/direct' and کرد = past 'made/did', so together 'arrayed/marshaled'.
segments.s667-g422: خروشان is an adverb/adjectival participle 'roaring, tumultuous' placed before the verb for emphasis. بیامد is past 'came'. ز جای نبرد is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the place of battle' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place/spot', نبرد = 'battle'), so 'came roaring from the battlefield.'
segments.s667-g423: بغلتید is a poetic/archaic past plural verb meaning 'they rolled/tumbled' (here describing prostration or throwing themselves on the ground). در پیش یزدان is a locative phrase 'in the presence of God' (پیش = before/in the presence of, یزدان = God/divine). به خاک adds direction/result 'to the ground' (literally 'to dust/earth').
segments.s667-g424: همی گفت is a habitual or iterative past/past‑narrative form 'he/they kept saying' (همی marks continuous or iterative aspect in classical style; گفت is 'said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O'. داور داد و پاک is a vocative/tag describing the addressee: داور = 'judge/arbiter' with judicial sense (here divine), داد = 'justice' (an attributive noun 'just'), و پاک = 'and pure' — together 'O Judge, just and pure.'
segments.s667-g281: که introduces a clause of extent/result ('that'); چندان is an intensifier 'so much/so' (to that extent). کند here is the verb 'to do/make' used with the verbal noun שتاب (a common classical/Persian periphrasis) — the verb precedes the subject (سگ) in this poetical word order. به تیزی is an adverbial phrase 'with speed/keenness' where به + noun yields an adverbial ('by/with -ly'), and شتاب is the verbal noun/infinitive 'haste, to hasten' functioning as the action modified by چندان and به تیزی.
segments.s667-g282: که (again) introduces the result clause 'that'; از + کام او is a genitive/ablative phrase 'from his reach/desire' (او = 'his'); دورتر is the comparative 'farther' and باشد is the subjunctive/present-form of 'to be' placed before the nominative (آب) in poetic inversion — آب is the logical subject/topic 'the water'. The whole clause = 'that the water is/lies farther from his reach.'
segments.s667-g283: ببردند is a past/plural verb 'they took' (here a perfective/past action); دیوان is the agent/subject (here meaning hostile supernatural beings in context — not one of the listed senses, see senses mapping). دلت is دل + second-person suffix ت 'your heart' (مَرَک/مَرَا split as archaic enclitic) and را is the definite/object marker. ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the way/path' giving source — together: 'they took your heart from the path' (i.e. led you astray).
segments.s667-g284: که here gives the reason/explanation 'for' or 'because'; نزدیک شاه آمدی is a simple clause 'you came near the king' with آمدی as second-person past 'you came'. رزمخواه is an appositive/vocative 'O war-seeker' or 'O aspiring fighter' addressing the addressee after the clause.
segments.s667-g285: بپیچی is a present/subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن 'to turn/twist' with the b- prefix used in classical/poetic Persian; here it yields the sense 'you (may) be turned/you turn aside'. ز marks the origin 'by/from', باد افره is 'the wind of the maple' (افره = maple), and ایزدی is an adjective 'divine' modifying افره (or the wind) — together: 'you are turned aside by the divine maple-wind.'
segments.s667-g286: هم 'also/likewise' adds an additional object of turning aside; از introduces the cause/source 'from' and کرده is a noun/participle 'what is done' or 'deeds', followed by و کارهای بدی 'and evil works/deeds' — the phrase lists things the person is turned away from or estranged from.
segments.s667-g287: دگر here means 'another' or 'furthermore'; آنک is a relative/connector 'that which' or 'that (which)'; گفتی in the line is the verb 'you said' (the provided senses do not include this verbal reading, see senses mapping). مرا is the object 'to me' (split in the segmentation as م + را); کهترند is a predicate in the plural 'they are lesser/ inferior' (کهتر + -ند). The clause as a whole: 'another thing that you told me is: they are lesser.'
segments.s667-g288: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones' (great men); که introduces a relative 'who/that'; با ... و با ... are repeated prepositional phrases 'with ... and with ...' (the repetition is emphatic or formulaic). طوق and افسر are regalia/armorial items (a collar/neck ornament and a helmet/head-piece) — افسرند carries the plural ending -ند and here denotes that those great ones 'are with' or 'have' collar and helmet (i.e. are adorned/armored).
segments.s667-g353: مَده (مده) is a negative imperative meaning “do not give” addressed to the addressee (imperative with short-form negation). جان ایرانیان را is the definite direct object marked by را (“the lives of the Iranians”). به باد is a prepositional idiom “to the wind” (باد = wind), i.e. “to ruin/squander.”
segments.s667-g354: نگه کن is an imperative “keep/guard” (2nd‑person) that continues the instruction. بدین here functions as a dative-like particle phrase meaning “to/for these,” followed by نامداران “the famed/renowned ones.” به داد is an adverbial phrase “with justice” (داد = justice), specifying how they should be entrusted or preserved.
segments.s667-g355: ز is the classical preposition “out of/from.” مردی here is abstract “manliness” (an ethical quality) rather than a concrete man. ببخشای is an imperative/subjunctive meaning “pardon/spare.” بر جان خویش is a phrase “for his life / concerning his life,” where خویش (split as خوی + ش) is the possessive “his own.” Together: “spare (him) out of manliness, for his life.”
segments.s667-g356: که is the conjunction “that/for,” introducing the explanatory clause. هرگز is the negative adverb “never.” نیامد is the past negative “did not come / did not occur.” چنین کار پیش literally “such a deed before” (چنین = such, کار = deed/affair, پیش = before/in former times). The whole clause: “for such a thing never came before.”
segments.s667-g357: بدو is a fused dative = “to him/unto him.” گفت here is the reporting verb “said” (3rd person past). بهرام is the speaker’s name (subject). کز is the relative/conjunction “that/from which,” introducing the content clause. شهرِ تو is “your city” (شهر = city, تو = your).
segments.s667-g358: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase “from the world” (ز = from, گیتی = the world). نیامد is the past negative “did not come.” جزین is the restrictive particle “except these/except this.” بهر تو is a beneficiary phrase “for you / on your behalf.” Altogether: “From the world no one came for you except these.”
segments.s667-g359: که introduces the clause continuing Bahram’s remark. ماهی فروشند is a verbal phrase meaning “they sell fish / they are fish-sellers” (فروشند = archaic 3rd‑person plural of ‘sell’). یکسر همه is an intensifying adverbial “utterly/entirely—all of them.” The phrase says these men are wholly fish-sellers.
segments.s667-g360: به تموز is a temporal prepositional phrase “to/at Tammuz” (a month-name). تا is the conjunction/preposition “until.” روزگار دمه is a temporal phrase “the time/moment of the breath” (روزگار = time/season, دمه = breath/moment), giving the limit of the period; together they mark the span during which they sell fish.
segments.s667-g337: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic -ش meaning 'his army'. سراسر here is an adverbial modifier 'entire/throughout' that intensifies شکسته. شکسته شدی is an archaic/poetic past result clause: شکسته (past participle 'broken') + شد(ی) (past copula) = 'was broken/was shattered'. The clause as a whole = 'his army was completely broken.'
segments.s667-g338: This clause describes the state of the routes after the defeat. راه is the noun 'road/route' and بی‌راه here describes disorder or being off the proper way ('wrong/off-road'); بسته شدی uses the same archaic past copula pattern 'was closed/was blocked'. برو is a short, poetic-form verb/adverb here linked to the motion/state of the route (rendered in translation as 'the routes fell into confusion'); the whole phrase means that the paths became disorderly and were shut/blocked.
segments.s667-g339: همی‌خواسته is an archaic/poetic past-continuous or experiential form meaning 'had been wanting' or 'was wishing/asking for'. از یلان is a prepositional phrase 'from the heroes/warriors' and زینهار functions as a noun 'a warning/admonition' (object of the desire). Word order reflects classical Persian: verb (compound) + prepositional object.
segments.s667-g340: پیاده بماندی is a past-form predicate 'remained on foot' (پیاده = 'on foot'; بماندی = past 'remained' with the -ی past ending typical of epic Persian). نبودیش یار is an existential/possessive phrase: نبودیش = 'there was not for him' / 'his lack', with the enclitic -ش marking 'his'; یار = 'companion/ally'. Together: 'he remained on foot; he had no companion.'
segments.s667-g341: غمی شد is a short phrase meaning 'grief arose' (غمی = 'a grief', شد = 'became/occurred'). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when', and از خواب بیدار شد is the past clause 'he awoke from sleep' (بیدار شد = 'became awake'). The order is typical: main event + temporal subordinate introduced by چو.
segments.s667-g342: This is a compact, poetic description: سر پر هنر is a nominal phrase 'a head full of skill' (سر = 'head'; پر = 'full (of)'; هنر = 'skill/virtue'). پر ز تیمار شد is predicative: 'became full of care/solicitude' (پر = 'full', ز = 'of/from', تیمار = 'care/worry', شد = 'became'). The repetition of پر is a poetic device (X full of Y ... full of Z).
segments.s667-g343: شب تیره is 'the dark night' (adjective after noun); با درد و غم = 'with pain and sorrow' (با marking accompaniment). بود is the past copula 'was', and جفت here functions as a predicate meaning 'paired' or 'companion/alongside' (i.e. the night was accompanied by grief). The clause states that the night was matched by pain and sorrow.
segments.s667-g344: بپوشید takes the object آن خواب literally 'he put on/covered that sleep' — a poetic way to say he wrapped himself in sleep or hid himself under that sleep (بپوشید = past transitive 'covered/assumed/put on' used figuratively). و با کس نگفت is a coordinated clause 'and (he) said/told it to no one' — با کس = 'to/with anyone', نگفت = past negative 'did not tell' (here the verb is negative past 'he did not say' and with با کس means 'to nobody/with no one did he speak').
segments.s667-g265: This is an archaic word order for expressing gratitude: 'مرا' marks the experiencer ('to me'), 'از تو' gives the cause ('because of you'), 'آنگاه' is a temporal adverb ('then'), and 'بودی سپاس' is an older copular construction meaning 'there was gratitude' (past). Put together: 'There was gratitude in me because of you then' = 'I was then grateful to you.'
segments.s667-g266: The verb 'خواندمی' (past/perfect) means 'I declared/hailed/called'; the phrase 'تو را' puts 'you' in topical/emphatic position (object/target). The following nouns 'شاه و نیکی‌شناس' are a compound predicate: 'king and knower/recognizer of goodness' — i.e. 'I called you king and one who recognizes goodness.'
segments.s667-g267: 'که' introduces the content clause. Word order is object–indirect object–verb: 'دختر (object) به من (to me) داده‌ای' — 'you gave (have given) a daughter to me.' 'آن زمان' is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb.
segments.s667-g268: This continues the temporal/qualifying clause: 'که از تخت ایران نبردی گمان' — 'when/that you had the thought/plan about taking the throne of Iran.' 'از تخت ایران' is the prepositional object ('from/of the throne of Iran'), 'نبردی' is the past action 'you seized/assumed' (here 'seize/win' the throne), and 'گمان' ('thought/idea/assumption') names the mental act tied to the verb in this older syntax.
segments.s667-g269: 'فرستاده‌ای' is the perfect/past of 'to send' ('you sent/you have sent') addressed to the addressee. The noun phrase 'گنج آراسته' ('an adorned/ornamented treasure') is the direct object of that verb.
segments.s667-g270: This phrase gives the destination/recipients and the additional items: 'به نزدیک من' = 'to/near me', then 'دختر و خواسته' lists what was brought/asked — 'the daughter and the request' (here 'خواسته' functions as a past participle/adjectival noun 'asked/desired'), so the clause means roughly 'you brought the daughter to me and asked (for her).' Word order is somewhat elliptical and poetic.
segments.s667-g271: 'چو' here means 'when.' The clause 'چو من دوست بودی' = 'when you were my friend' (notice the inverted order: 'چو' + experiencer + predicate). 'به ایران' specifies the place 'in Iran.' The final 'تو را' topicalizes/emphasizes 'you' (dative/accusative/topic marker).
segments.s667-g272: The phrase begins with an implied negation (poetic order here omits a separate 'نه' token in the provided list). 'رزم آمدی' = 'battle/war came' (literally 'war arrived'); in older Persian this can be used impersonally ('war befell'). 'با دلیران' = 'with/against brave ones' and the trailing 'تو را' again topicalizes 'you' (dative/emphatic). Together: 'No fighting befell you with your brave men' (i.e. 'you did not have to fight, with your warriors').
segments.s667-g225: بیابی is a 2nd‑person verb here introducing what the addressee will discover (‘you will find’). It is followed by a locative phrase به نزدیک من (‘near me’) and the noun مهتری as the thing discovered (preeminence); word order is verb → locative → complement, a common poetic inversion where the verb heads a clause that reports the expected result.
segments.s667-g226: شوی is the 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of شدن meaning ‘you become’. بی‌نیاز از X is a fixed adjective + preposition construction meaning ‘free from X,’ so بی‌نیاز از بد کهتری is the predicate: ‘free from the badness of being lesser.’ کهتری is a nominal abstract (‘inferiority/lesser status’) governed by از.
segments.s667-g227: چوکشته is the conditional/temporal compound (چو + کشته) meaning ‘when/if (he is) slain’; شود is the present/passive auxiliary ‘is/shall be’ forming the passive ‘is slain’. شاه ایران is the subject and به جنگ is a locative phrase ‘in battle’.
segments.s667-g228: تو را is the beneficiary/topic marked with را (here rendered ‘to you’), and آید is the intransitive verb ‘comes/will come’. آن تاج و تختش is the nominative phrase ‘that crown and his throne’ (تخت‑ش = his throne). به چنگ is an idiom meaning ‘into the grasp/hand’ (چنگ literally ‘claw/hold’), so the whole clause means ‘that crown and his throne will come into your hand.’
segments.s667-g229: وزان (= وز آن) is a compound prepositional phrase ‘from that/thereupon’ marking source; جایگه means ‘place/position’. من is the explicit subject and شوم is 1st‑person (subjunctive/optative) form of شدن here used as ‘I shall go/set out’ in poetry; سوی روم gives the destination ‘toward Rome’.
segments.s667-g230: تو را marks the beneficiary/indirect object (‘for you’), and مانم (from ماندن) is 1st‑person present/subjunctive ‘I leave/remain’; here it means ‘I leave (to) you’ or ‘I will let remain for you’. این لشکر و گنج و بوم is the list of items left to the addressee: ‘this army and treasure and homeland.’ The word order places the beneficiary before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s667-g231: ازان (‘from that’/’on that account’) introduces the reason; گفتم is the speaker’s past verb ‘I said’ (reported speech). این کم پسند آمدی is a reported remark: این (‘this’) modified by کم‑پسند (‘little‑pleasing’, i.e. displeasing or not much liked) and آمدی ‘you came/you have come’ — together conveying ‘This is not pleasing; you have come little‑esteemed.’
segments.s667-g232: بدین کارها = ب + دین + کارها functions as ‘into/onto these affairs’ (بدین is an idiomatic compound ‘to/into these’), where کارها is the plural ‘affairs/deeds’. فرمند here conveys the idea of a command/ordering (from the root meaning ‘to command’), so فرمند آمدی means ‘you came at someone’s command’ or ‘you entered these matters by command’. آمدی again is the 2nd‑person past/present perfective ‘you came’/’you entered’.
segments.s667-g249: کسی را is topical/emphatic 'someone' with the postposed را; که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). آید is 3rd-person present used poetically for 'befalls/happens to'; زمانه به سر is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ('the time/fortune upon the head'), so the whole segment means 'someone upon whom time/fate comes'.
segments.s667-g250: ز مردم 'from the people' is a poetic prepositional phrase; به گفتار uses به to mark means ('by/through speech'); جوید is a present (poetic) 3rd-person verb 'seeks/searches for' and هنر is its direct object 'merit/virtue/excellence'. Word order places the means and source before the verb, typical in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s667-g251: شنیدم is the 1st-person past 'I heard' (used here as a simple past narrative). سخنهای ناسودمند is the direct object: سخنهای (plural 'words/talks') qualified by ناسودمند 'unprofitable/useless'.
segments.s667-g252: دلی گشته ترسان is a small clause: دلی (a heart) + گشته (perfect/participial 'has become') + ترسان (predicate adjective 'fearful/afraid'). ز بیم گزند is a poetic source phrase 'from the fear of harm' with ز marking cause/origin and گزند 'harm'.
