segments.s667-g253: یکی آنک here means 'one (case/person) that…' with آنک = آن که 'that which/that who'. گفتی in the poem functions as reported speech ('you said …'); کشم is an embedded verb form ('I should/would take/bring/kill' depending on context) and شاه را is the definite object 'the king' (را marks the object). (Note: some dictionary senses supplied do not list the exact verbal forms used here, so the entry senses below reflect the closest available or are marked -1 if none fit exactly.)
segments.s667-g254: سپارم is a 1st-person verb in a jussive/intentive form 'I will entrust/leave' (classical Persian often uses this form to express intent). به تو marks the indirect object ('to you'); لشکر و گاه را is the coordinated direct object 'the army and the camp/time' with را as the object marker placed after the coordination.
segments.s667-g255: یکی داستان زد 'one (time) a tale he struck/told' — زد here is the simple past of زدن used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'told/related a story'. برین is orthographic for بر این 'about this/on this', so برین مرد مه = 'about that great/valiant man'. This is a compressed epic phrasing: object (داستان) + verb (زد) + prepositional phrase (بر این مرد مه).
segments.s667-g256: که introduces a subordinate clause 'in which/that', درویش را is the (emphasized) object 'the dervish', چون here means 'how' (introducing the manner or question), برانی is used as a participial/verb form meaning 'you drove away/expelled' (classical poetic form), and ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. Together: 'how you drove the dervish from the (village)'.
segments.s667-g233: سپه تاختن دانی و کیمیا is a compact poetic clause where the infinitive تاختن (to charge) precedes the finite verb دانی (you know); Persian often places an infinitive before a verb of perception/knowledge. دانی is the second-person singular present of دانستن (‘you know’). و links کيميا (here used metaphorically as a ‘secret art’/elixir) to the preceding predicate, so the clause reads literally “you know (سپَه) to charge and (know) the secret art.”
segments.s667-g234: سپهبد به دستت پدر‌گر نیا is elliptical and idiomatic. به دستت (به + دست + enclitic -ت) means ‘in your hand / in your power’ (دست here = control/power). پدرگر is a compound adjective ‘father‑like / fatherly’. نیا is used imperatively/elliptically as a prohibition (classical negative imperative ‘do not…’) so the whole phrase reads roughly ‘the spahbed is in your hand — do not be fatherly (i.e. indulgent).’
segments.s667-g235: ز ما این نه گفتار آرایشست literally 'from us this not‑speech is ornament.' ز ما = 'from us'; the intervening نه functions as a negation/contrast marker ('not / not merely') modifying گفتار. آرایشست (آرایش + -ست) is a predicative nominal, 'it is an ornament/embellishment.' The word order (topic 'ز ما' up front, predicate at the end) is typical of epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s667-g236: مرا بر تو بر، جای بخشایشست is a somewhat compressed clause: م (here a contracted/clitic form of هست used existentially) plus را (object/topic marker) give مـرا 'there is for me / as for me'. بر تو بر repeats the preposition بر for emphasis ('with regard to you / toward you'). جای بخشایشست is the predicate 'a place/room of forgiveness exists.' Altogether: 'As for me, toward you there is room for pardon.'
segments.s667-g237: بدین روز = به + این (classical poetic 'on this/that day'); روز = 'day'. با introduces an accompanying instrument/means: با خوارمایه سپاه = 'with a contemptible / low‑worth army' — خوارمایه functions as an adjective phrase qualifying سپاه. The whole prepositional phrase sets the circumstance 'on that day, with a low‑value force'.
segments.s667-g238: برابر یکی ساختی رزمگاه is a verb phrase with object at the end (typical Persian V‑final in poetry): ساختی is second‑person singular past 'you made/appointed'. برابر یکی = 'equal to one' / 'a match for one'; here برابر = 'equal / matching' and رزمگاه ('battlefield') is the thing fashioned/established. So: 'you made the battlefield a match (equal) to one (army).' The sense: with poor troops you held the field as if equal to a regular force.
segments.s667-g239: نیابی جز این نیز پیغام من: نیابی is ن + یابی (second‑person form of یافتن/یافتن), here with negative prefix ن- meaning 'you will not find' (in classical usage can carry a future/irrealis sense). جز این نیز = 'except this / besides this' (نیز adds the sense 'also / even so'); پیغام من = 'my message.' Word order places the negative verb before the exemptive phrase for emphasis: 'you will find nothing except this message of mine.'
segments.s667-g240: اگر سربپیچانی از کام من is a conditional clause. اگر = 'if'. سربپیچانی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of سربپیچیدن ('to turn the head aside') used idiomatically 'to turn away/evade/refuse.' از + کام من = 'from my desire/will.' So: 'if you turn aside from my wish' (i.e. if you refuse my request).

sentences.s667-s217: The roaring ones rose up from that place; one (of them) with a cow-bodied mace in his hand.
sentences.s667-s57: An ambitious (world-seeking) man should not seek valor by profitless words and folly.
sentences.s667-s58: The proud envoy came again near Bahram.
sentences.s667-s59: That chooser delivered his message and said: 'Truly, that speech will do him ill (will not satisfy his desire).'
sentences.s667-s60: When the man heard, the speaker said that an answer should not be hidden from the chief.
sentences.s667-s17: I have come here to fight with an army; I left Baghdad by the king's command.
sentences.s667-s18: When news came from the army of the king of Sāveh to that resplendent court,

sentences.s667-s82: In that cramped spot they drew up a rank; the sky turned blue and the earth vanished.
sentences.s667-s83: There was an army on the right—forty thousand strong—so that the place grew tight for the dagger-bearer.
sentences.s667-s84: Those very forty thousand men of the brave ones then placed themselves at the rear of his army.
sentences.s667-s29: He said to you, “Turn your face — take the road toward me”; you heard his disagreeable speech.
sentences.s667-s30: If a mountain should come my way on the road, I will set it beneath my feet with elephants and an army.
sentences.s667-s31: When Bahram heard his words, he laughed at the sharpness of them.
sentences.s667-s32: He gave this answer: that if the king of the world seeks my death in secret…
sentences.s667-s165: He resolved to wage war with his army in this manner until the world turned black.
sentences.s667-s166: The Turks and the winged multitudes were laid low; the world became such that the quest for the world was free.
sentences.s667-s167: When Bahram, the warrior, slept in his tent, all night his heart was paired with battle.
sentences.s667-s168: Bahram saw such a lion in his dream that the Turks became brave to fight him.
sentences.s667-s25: After that he sent an aged man to Bahram's presence, a master of speech.
sentences.s667-s26: He said to him, 'Speak Persian; here, do not pour water on the staring face.'
sentences.s667-s27: Know this indeed to be the true cause: from this king of yours your death sprang.
sentences.s667-s28: He sent (him) to your battle a man who has no equal in the world.
sentences.s667-s97: There is weaponry, a mule-stable and a curtained chamber — more besides, for thought can bring them into place.
sentences.s667-s98: If he counts the horses and the men of the waste and the mountain, he too will become weary.
sentences.s667-s99: All the kings are lesser to me — if they themselves accept inferiority.
sentences.s667-s100: If you were to become the flowing water of the sea — and if the mountain had a foot, it would run.
sentences.s667-s13: When the Chinese phaghfur saw him, he charged, urging the world‑renowned steed into its accustomed pace.
